Run it once — for user 1 (who has 2 logins and 3 sessions), you'll see `logins = 6`, not 2. Explain (in your head) the row-multiplication, then write the CORRECT query that returns `id, name, logins, sessions` with the genuine counts (no double-counting from the join). Order by id.
questions.starter:
-- Return id, name, logins, sessions with genuine (non-inflated) counts.
-- Use COUNT(DISTINCT ...) on the right unique keys, or pre-aggregate.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT u.id, u.name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT l.id) AS logins,
       COUNT(DISTINCT s.id) AS sessions
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN logins   l ON u.id = l.user_id
LEFT JOIN sessions s ON u.id = s.user_id
GROUP BY u.id, u.name
ORDER BY u.id;
key_points: Two LEFT JOINs on the same key (u.id) Cartesian-multiply: Alice's 2 logins × 3 sessions = 6 join rows
COUNT(*) and COUNT(DISTINCT non-unique-col) get inflated; COUNT(DISTINCT primary_key) on each side does not
Alternative fix: pre-aggregate each table to one row per user in a CTE, then join the totals — generally clearer than DISTINCT counts

options: id 3 — this is the LEFT JOIN anti-join idiom for 'rows on the left with no match on the right'
id 1 and 2 — customers who DO have orders
All three rows — the WHERE is a no-op for a LEFT JOIN
Empty result
questions.explanation: This is the 'anti-join' pattern. The LEFT JOIN produces (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, NULL). The WHERE `o.customer_id IS NULL` keeps ONLY the unmatched rows (i.e. customers with no order). Equivalent to `WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE customer_id = c.id)`. Important not to confuse with `WHERE o.customer_id = X`, which performs the opposite collapse described in sql-210.

options: It is dropped — because b.c_id is NULL, the second LEFT JOIN finds no match and the row is filtered
(3, NULL) — LEFT JOINs preserve a's rows; both b and c are NULL
An error — NULL cannot participate in a join condition
questions.explanation: Each LEFT JOIN preserves the left side. After the first LEFT JOIN, row a=3 has b.a_id and b.c_id NULL. The second LEFT JOIN's ON clause `b.c_id = c.id` becomes `NULL = c.id`, which is NULL for every c-row (never TRUE), so no match — but because it is a LEFT JOIN, a's row survives with all c columns NULL. Result: (3, NULL). LEFT JOINs only DROP rows when you later filter the right side in WHERE.

questions.prompt:
Given:
```sql
SELECT u.id, COUNT(*) AS interactions
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
LEFT JOIN sessions s ON u.id = s.user_id
GROUP BY u.id;
```
Assume one user has 2 orders and 3 sessions. What does `COUNT(*)` return for that user?
options: 5 (the orders plus the sessions)
3 (the larger of the two)
6 (the CROSS PRODUCT of orders and sessions — 2 * 3)
1 (one row per user)
questions.explanation: Both LEFT JOINs are on the same key (u.id). The first produces 2 rows (one per order). The second then matches each of those rows against ALL 3 sessions, giving 2 × 3 = 6 rows. COUNT(*) reports 6. This is the 'Cartesian explosion through chained one-to-many joins' bug. Aggregate the orders and sessions separately first, then join the totals together, or use `COUNT(DISTINCT o.id)` and `COUNT(DISTINCT s.id)` in the same SELECT.

questions.prompt:
Given:
```sql
CREATE TABLE sales (d TEXT, amt INT);
INSERT INTO sales VALUES
  ('2025-01-01', 50), ('2025-01-01', 100), ('2025-01-02', 30);

SELECT d, amt,
       SUM(amt) OVER (ORDER BY d) AS running_total
FROM sales
ORDER BY d, amt;
```
What does `running_total` look like for the two rows with d = '2025-01-01'?
options: 50 and 150 — a strict row-by-row running total
150 for BOTH rows — the default frame is RANGE, which lumps tied rows together
150 and 50 — ordering is reversed
NULL for both — ties make the result undefined
questions.explanation: THE classic window-function gotcha. When you write `SUM() OVER (ORDER BY x)` and omit the frame, ANSI's default is `RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`. RANGE is value-based: 'CURRENT ROW' means 'all rows whose ORDER BY value equals mine' — i.e. all peers. So both Jan-01 rows are 'tied,' and both report the SAME running total: 50+100 = 150. To get a strict per-row running total, write `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` AND add a tie-breaker to ORDER BY.

options: '2025-01-01' — same as the current row when there is no previous
'1970-01-01' — the engine substitutes the epoch
NULL — there is no previous row; LAG returns NULL by default
An error — LAG cannot be called on the first row
questions.explanation: LAG returns NULL for any row that has no predecessor in the window/partition. Most engineers forget to handle this and get NULL polluting downstream subtraction (e.g. `ts - LAG(ts) OVER (...)` returns NULL for the first row, then the team complains the chart 'starts blank'). Use the optional second/third arguments: `LAG(ts, 1, ts) OVER (...)` substitutes the current value as a default; or COALESCE the result.

options: 40 — the last value in the partition
20 — because the default frame is `RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`, so `LAST_VALUE` sees only rows up to the current one
30 — one row ahead
NULL — LAST_VALUE is undefined without an explicit frame
questions.explanation: FIRST_VALUE looks right; LAST_VALUE almost never does. When you write `OVER (ORDER BY d)` and omit the frame, the default is `RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` — so the frame slides and `LAST_VALUE` returns the value of the CURRENT row, not the partition's actual last row. To get the partition's last value you must write `OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING)` (or use MAX/LAG-with-IGNORE-NULLS depending on intent).

options: (5, 3) — COUNT(v) ignores NULLs even as a window aggregate
(3, 3) — COUNT(*) over the window excludes NULLs in the partition expression
(5, NULL)
questions.explanation: Aggregates-as-window-functions have the SAME NULL behavior as their ordinary form: `COUNT(*)` counts every row in the window (5), and `COUNT(col)` skips NULLs (3). Many engineers assume the windowed form 'just counts what's there' — it doesn't, NULL handling is unchanged. Mirror trap for SUM/AVG: `SUM(v) OVER ()` skips NULLs; `AVG(v) OVER ()` shrinks both numerator and denominator.

questions.prompt:
Given (Snowflake / Postgres):
```sql
SELECT user_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id) AS rn
FROM events;
```
What is true about this query?
options: It assigns rn=1 to all rows in each partition
Because there is no ORDER BY inside OVER(), the row numbering within each partition is non-deterministic across runs
It errors — ROW_NUMBER requires ORDER BY
It assigns globally unique numbers ignoring the PARTITION BY
questions.explanation: ROW_NUMBER without an ORDER BY inside OVER() is legal in most engines, but the order in which rows receive numbers is implementation-defined and can change between runs (parallel scans, plan changes, storage layout). This is a silent correctness bug: 'give me the latest event per user' written as `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id) = 1` will sometimes return the right row and sometimes not. Always specify ORDER BY (and a tie-breaker if values can tie).

questions.prompt:
Given:
```sql
SELECT v FROM (VALUES (1),(1),(2)) a(v)
UNION
SELECT v FROM (VALUES (1),(3)) b(v);
```
(Or two physical tables with the same data.) How many rows does UNION return?
options: 5 — UNION concatenates and keeps all rows
4 — UNION dedupes only within each side, not across the combined result
3 — UNION dedupes the FULL combined result (1, 2, 3 distinct)
1 — UNION returns only common rows
questions.explanation: UNION applies DISTINCT to the ENTIRE combined output — both within-side duplicates AND across-side duplicates collapse. Many engineers think UNION = 'UNION ALL plus dedup of just the second side' — it doesn't, it dedupes the whole result. So {1,1,2} ∪ {1,3} -> {1, 2, 3} = 3 rows. UNION ALL would have returned all 5 rows. If you want 'add the second source without disturbing duplicates already in the first,' you have to write it explicitly.

questions.prompt:
Given (Postgres / SQLite):
```sql
WITH a(v) AS (VALUES (1),(1),(1),(2)),
     b(v) AS (VALUES (1))
SELECT v FROM a EXCEPT SELECT v FROM b ORDER BY v;
```
What does this return?
options: 1, 2 (the duplicates of 1 remain on the left)
Just 2 — EXCEPT is set-based: any v=1 in b removes ALL v=1 from a, regardless of multiplicity
1, 1, 2 (two of the three 1's on the left are removed, leaving one)
Empty
questions.explanation: Standard SQL's EXCEPT is set semantics (dedup): if ANY occurrence of v exists in b, ALL occurrences in a are removed. So {1,1,1,2} EXCEPT {1} = {2}. If you want multiset semantics (remove one occurrence per match), use `EXCEPT ALL` (supported by Postgres). MySQL and SQL Server have their own quirks here. The naive expectation that EXCEPT 'subtracts row by row' is wrong.

options: It filters out 'phantom' empty groups
It is a no-op — every group that exists by definition has at least one row, so COUNT(*) > 0 is always TRUE
It changes the optimizer's plan from grouping to streaming
It errors because HAVING requires a column reference
questions.explanation: GROUP BY only emits a group when at least one row exists for that key — there are no 'empty groups' in SQL. So `HAVING COUNT(*) > 0` is always TRUE and filters nothing. People often write this thinking it eliminates groups with all-NULL aggregates, but that requires `HAVING COUNT(col) > 0` (note the column, not *). Recognising no-op predicates is part of reading legacy SQL fluently.

options: 0 — missing ELSE breaks the sum
130 — the non-paid rows produce NULL, which SUM silently skips, so it works
200 — sums every row's amt
NULL — a single NULL in SUM poisons the result
questions.explanation: CASE without an ELSE returns NULL for unmatched rows. SUM ignores NULLs, so the result is the sum over the matching rows only: 100 + 30 = 130. This pattern is idiomatic and correct — BUT it depends on aggregate-NULL semantics, which is why some engineers reflexively add `ELSE 0`. The Postgres-style alternative is even cleaner: `SUM(amt) FILTER (WHERE status = 'paid')`.

questions.prompt:
Given (Postgres):
```sql
CREATE TABLE events (id INT, kind TEXT);
INSERT INTO events VALUES (1,NULL),(2,'click'),(3,NULL),(4,'view');

SELECT
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE kind IS NULL)            AS f_null,
  SUM(CASE WHEN kind IS NULL THEN 1 END)          AS s_no_else,
  SUM(CASE WHEN kind IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)   AS s_with_else
FROM events;
```
Which pair of values is correct?
options: f_null=2, s_no_else=2, s_with_else=2
f_null=0, s_no_else=NULL, s_with_else=0 (FILTER ignores NULL inputs)
f_null=4, s_no_else=2, s_with_else=2
f_null=NULL, s_no_else=NULL, s_with_else=NULL
questions.explanation: The FILTER clause is just a per-row predicate — it doesn't itself reject NULL inputs; it just keeps rows where the WHERE-style predicate is TRUE. The IS NULL predicate is itself NULL-safe and TRUE for two rows. So all three expressions correctly return 2. (Contrast: `COUNT(kind) FILTER (...)` would skip NULL kinds because of the column COUNT, not because of FILTER.) Knowing this distinction matters when migrating SUM(CASE) to FILTER.

options: 1, 2, 3 (NULLs are ignored in IN)
2 only — the NULL in the subquery makes the IN test return NULL (not TRUE) for non-matching outer rows, so they are filtered
Empty result (NULL poisons IN entirely)
Error — NULL is not allowed in IN list
questions.explanation: Subtle asymmetry between IN and NOT IN. For IN, a TRUE match still wins: `2 IN (2, NULL)` is TRUE because 2=2 returns TRUE and `TRUE OR NULL = TRUE`. But for non-matching outer rows, e.g. `1 IN (2, NULL)`, the expression evaluates to `FALSE OR NULL = NULL` — not TRUE — so the row is dropped. Net effect with no matches: just 2. Same shape as in production: IN behaves correctly for matches, but loses non-matches that should have been kept by other predicates. NOT IN (sql-202) is the more catastrophic version.

options: Only id=1 (the one customer with orders)
All three customers — EXISTS is true if the subquery returns ANY row, regardless of correlation
Empty result — EXISTS requires a correlated predicate
An error — missing join condition
questions.explanation: EXISTS just checks whether the subquery returns at least one row. There is no correlation here (no `WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.id`), so the subquery is evaluated once, returns 2 rows, and the predicate is TRUE for every outer row. Beginners assume EXISTS automatically correlates by foreign-key proximity — it doesn't. You must spell the correlation out explicitly.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes integer division behavior in SQL Server / PostgreSQL / SQLite for `SELECT 3 / 2`?
options: Returns 1.5 because SQL automatically promotes integer division to floating point
Returns 1 — both operands are integers, so the result is truncated to an integer. Use 3.0 / 2 or CAST to get 1.5
Returns 2 (rounded)
Errors with a 'precision lost' warning
questions.explanation: When both operands of `/` are integers, most major engines (Postgres, SQL Server, SQLite, Oracle in many configurations) perform integer division and truncate toward zero. So `3 / 2 = 1`, not 1.5. Force floating-point by writing one operand as a decimal (`3.0/2`) or by casting (`CAST(3 AS NUMERIC)/2`). MySQL is the exception: it always returns a decimal for `/`. This bites computed columns like `total_paid * 100 / total_orders` returning 0% or 100%.

questions.prompt:
Given (MySQL with default settings):
```sql
SELECT 'abc' = 0;
```
What does this return in MySQL?
options: 0 (FALSE) — string and number are incomparable
1 (TRUE) — MySQL silently coerces 'abc' to numeric, which becomes 0
NULL — type mismatch yields unknown
An error — invalid comparison
questions.explanation: MySQL silently coerces strings to numbers in numeric comparisons. Any string that doesn't start with a digit converts to 0, so `'abc' = 0` is TRUE. Production nightmare: a query like `WHERE user_id = some_text_value` will match every row where user_id = 0. PostgreSQL would have errored on the type mismatch. Strict SQL modes in MySQL can prevent this, but they are not default in all versions / configs.

questions.prompt:
Given (PostgreSQL — `occurred_at` is TIMESTAMP):
```sql
CREATE TABLE events (id INT, occurred_at TIMESTAMP);
INSERT INTO events VALUES
  (1, '2025-01-15 09:00:00'),
  (2, '2025-01-31 09:00:00'),
  (3, '2025-01-31 23:45:00');

SELECT id FROM events
WHERE occurred_at BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31';
```
Which rows are returned?
options: All three — they all fall in January
1 only — rows 2 and 3 are excluded because the upper bound '2025-01-31' coerces to '2025-01-31 00:00:00', and any later time on Jan 31 exceeds that bound
1 and 2 only
Nothing — BETWEEN is exclusive on the right endpoint
questions.explanation: BETWEEN is inclusive on both endpoints — but a DATE literal coerces to a TIMESTAMP at midnight. So `BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31'` becomes `>= 2025-01-01 00:00:00 AND <= 2025-01-31 00:00:00`. Row 1 (Jan 15 09:00) passes. Rows 2 and 3 (Jan 31 with non-zero time component) are EXCLUDED because their times are strictly greater than midnight. Classic 'lost the last day of the month' bug. Fix: half-open `WHERE occurred_at >= '2025-01-01' AND occurred_at < '2025-02-01'`.

options: 1 — dates compare equal if same calendar day
NULL — the comparison is undefined
Errors because of type mismatch
questions.explanation: Equality comparison promotes the DATE literal to a TIMESTAMP at midnight. `'2025-03-15 14:30:00' = '2025-03-15 00:00:00'` is FALSE. People expect 'WHERE date_column = date_literal' to mean 'same calendar day' — it doesn't on a TIMESTAMP column. Fix: `WHERE DATE(occurred_at) = '2025-03-15'` (but that defeats an index on occurred_at), or the half-open range pattern.

questions.prompt: Given a B-tree index on `users(email)`. Which of the following predicates can the engine use the index to satisfy efficiently?
options: `WHERE LOWER(email) = 'alice@example.com'`
`WHERE email LIKE '%example.com'`
`WHERE email = 'alice@example.com'` (no function applied to the column)
`WHERE email || '' = 'alice@example.com'`
questions.explanation: An index on a column is usable only when the column appears 'bare' on one side of the predicate, the operator is sargable, and the right-hand side is a constant or another indexed expression. Wrapping in LOWER, leading `%` wildcards, or concatenating defeats the index because the optimizer cannot translate the predicate into a B-tree range scan. Fixes: store a normalized lowercase column, create an EXPRESSION INDEX on `LOWER(email)`, or use a TRIGRAM index for the leading-wildcard case.

questions.prompt:
Given:
```sql
SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT id FROM users ORDER BY created_at DESC
) sub;
```
Which statement is most accurate?
options: The outer query is guaranteed to return rows in created_at DESC order
The ORDER BY inside the subquery is often IGNORED by the optimizer; only a top-level ORDER BY guarantees ordering of the final result
It throws a syntax error in standard SQL
The outer query inherits the inner ORDER BY only if there is a LIMIT
questions.explanation: SQL is a set-oriented language; intermediate relations have no inherent order. An ORDER BY in a subquery / CTE is allowed but is only used internally (e.g. to feed a TOP/LIMIT, or to feed a window function). The optimizer may drop the sort altogether if it isn't needed. Many engineers rely on the inner ordering 'bubbling up' and watch it break the day the planner changes. Always put ORDER BY at the OUTER level when ordering of the result matters.

questions.prompt: You query a table and want the 10 most recent records: `SELECT * FROM events LIMIT 10;`. What is the bug?
options: LIMIT cannot be used without OFFSET
Without ORDER BY, the database returns ANY 10 rows — the result is non-deterministic and may change between runs
LIMIT 10 always returns the oldest 10, not the newest
The query is correct — LIMIT implicitly orders by insertion
questions.explanation: LIMIT without ORDER BY returns 10 arbitrary rows; the engine is free to return whatever it finds first. With a small table the same rows often come back, lulling you into thinking it works. After the table grows, parallelism changes, or a new index is added, the 'recent 10' silently becomes 'whatever 10 the scan picked first.' Always add `ORDER BY <col> DESC` to make 'top N' deterministic.

questions.prompt: An engineer runs `DELETE FROM orders;` (no WHERE clause). What happens in a standard ANSI/Postgres/MySQL setup with no extra safety hooks?
options: An error: 'DELETE without WHERE is not allowed'
All rows in `orders` are deleted; the statement returns the count of affected rows and commits if there is no transaction
Only the most recently inserted row is deleted
A warning is printed but nothing happens
questions.explanation: Plain SQL has no 'WHERE-required' safety. `DELETE FROM orders;` deletes every row. Same trap with `UPDATE orders SET status='X';` — no WHERE, every row changes. Some clients (MySQL Workbench, DataGrip) and tools have 'safe mode' switches; production runbooks usually require an explicit transaction so you can `ROLLBACK` if the row count surprises you. This is why senior engineers wrap destructive DML in a transaction even when they 'know' the WHERE is correct.

questions.prompt:
Given (PostgreSQL):
```sql
SELECT *
FROM orders
WHERE amt > ALL (SELECT amt FROM refunds WHERE status = 'rejected');
```
The `refunds` table currently has NO rows with `status = 'rejected'`. What does the outer query return?
options: Empty set — ALL over no rows is undefined
Every row in orders — ALL is vacuously TRUE when the subquery returns no rows
An error — cannot use ALL with an empty subquery
Only rows with amt = 0
questions.explanation: `x > ALL (empty)` is vacuously TRUE — there are no values to fail against, so by definition every x is 'greater than all of them.' Symmetric trap: `x > ANY (empty)` is vacuously FALSE. These quantifiers are mathematically consistent but counter-intuitive when the inner table happens to be empty (e.g. an unexpectedly filtered subquery). When NOT IN's subquery is empty you get a similar 'everything matches' surprise.

questions.prompt:
Given (PostgreSQL / standard SQL):
```sql
SELECT user_id,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts DESC) AS rn
FROM events
WHERE rn = 1;
```
Why does this fail?
options: rn is reserved
Window functions are evaluated AFTER the WHERE clause, so the alias `rn` does not yet exist when WHERE runs
PARTITION BY cannot be used with ROW_NUMBER
It does NOT fail — modern engines auto-correct
questions.explanation: SQL's logical evaluation order is FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT (including window functions) → ORDER BY. Window functions live in the SELECT phase, so neither WHERE nor the alias exist yet. Wrap in a subquery / CTE and filter outside, or use `QUALIFY` (Snowflake, BigQuery, Teradata, Databricks). This rule applies symmetrically to aggregates and aliases too.

questions.prompt:
You want the 'first signup event per user.' You write:
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT ON (user_id) user_id, event_type, ts
FROM events
ORDER BY user_id;
```
Which statement is correct?
options: It returns the chronologically first event per user — DISTINCT ON automatically picks the earliest row
It returns ONE row per user but the choice of which row is implementation-defined because `ts` is not in the ORDER BY
It errors — DISTINCT ON requires an aggregate
It is identical to GROUP BY user_id
questions.explanation: Postgres-specific gotcha: `DISTINCT ON (cols)` keeps the FIRST row per group as defined by ORDER BY. If ORDER BY does not include `ts` (or whatever you intend to break ties on), the row chosen is arbitrary — you may get any event per user, not the earliest. Correct form: `... ORDER BY user_id, ts ASC` to get the earliest, or `... ts DESC` for the latest. Same rule applies to ROW_NUMBER() + PARTITION BY without an ORDER BY tie-breaker.

questions.prompt:
Given:
```sql
CREATE TABLE events (user_id INT, kind TEXT);
INSERT INTO events VALUES
  (1,'click'),(1,'click'),(1,NULL),
  (2,NULL),(2,NULL);

SELECT user_id,
       COUNT(DISTINCT kind)                            AS d_naive,
       COUNT(DISTINCT COALESCE(kind, '<unset>'))       AS d_coalesce
FROM events
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id;
```
What does each user's `(d_naive, d_coalesce)` look like?
options: User 1: (1, 2) · User 2: (0, 1) — COUNT(DISTINCT kind) silently drops NULL; wrapping with COALESCE turns NULL into a real distinct value
User 1: (2, 2) · User 2: (1, 1) — NULL counts as a distinct kind by default
User 1: (1, 2) · User 2: (1, 1) — NULL is counted only when there are other values
questions.explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT expr) ignores rows where expr is NULL. User 1 has kinds {'click', 'click', NULL}; the NULL is skipped and only 'click' remains, giving 1 distinct. Wrapping with `COALESCE(kind, '<unset>')` turns NULL into the literal string '<unset>', which IS counted as a distinct value (so 'click' + '<unset>' = 2). User 2 is all NULL: d_naive = 0 (every row skipped); d_coalesce = 1 ('<unset>' is one distinct value). Critical for 'number of distinct event types per user' KPIs where NULL means a real-but-unlabeled event.

options: 'Y' — NULL equals NULL inside an ON clause
NULL — `a.k2 = b.k2` becomes `NULL = NULL` which is NULL, never TRUE, so the join fails and the LEFT side row is preserved with NULL on the right
Errors because NULL is not allowed in join keys
Empty result
questions.explanation: Even inside ON clauses, NULL = NULL is NULL, not TRUE. So when one of the join keys is NULL on both sides, the rows still don't match. The LEFT JOIN preserves the a-row with all b-columns NULL. This silently breaks 'composite key' joins in data warehouses where NULL is used as 'unknown.' Fixes: `a.k2 IS NOT DISTINCT FROM b.k2` (Postgres/SQLite), or COALESCE to a sentinel: `COALESCE(a.k2,-1) = COALESCE(b.k2,-1)`.

questions.prompt:
Given (SQLite/Postgres):
```sql
SELECT NULLIF(0, 0) AS a,
       1 / NULLIF(0, 0) AS b;
```
What does this return and why is the idiom useful?
options: a=0, b=0 — NULLIF returns 0 when the arguments match
a=NULL, b=NULL — NULLIF(x, x) returns NULL, which makes the division return NULL instead of raising a 'division by zero' error
a=1, b=1 — NULLIF acts like IS NULL
Throws 'division by zero' on the second column
questions.explanation: `NULLIF(x, y)` returns NULL when x = y and returns x otherwise. The standard 'safe division' idiom is `numerator / NULLIF(denominator, 0)` — the denominator becomes NULL when it would have been zero, and any arithmetic with NULL yields NULL instead of an error. Most engines treat division by NULL as NULL, not as an error. This avoids both crashes and ugly CASE wrappers.

questions.prompt:
Given (Postgres):
```sql
WITH cte AS (
  SELECT id, RANDOM() AS r FROM big_table
)
SELECT * FROM cte WHERE r < 0.5
UNION ALL
SELECT * FROM cte WHERE r >= 0.5;
```
Is the UNION ALL guaranteed to reproduce EXACTLY the rows in `big_table`?
options: Yes — the CTE is materialized once and the two halves partition the rows deterministically
Not necessarily — unless the CTE is materialized (and many planners now inline it by default), RANDOM() is evaluated independently in each reference, and some rows may land in BOTH halves or NEITHER
Yes — RANDOM() is deterministic within a transaction
No — UNION ALL always deduplicates
questions.explanation: Since Postgres 12, simple CTEs may be inlined into each reference, which means a non-deterministic function like RANDOM() is re-evaluated per reference — a row may have r=0.3 in the first reference and r=0.7 in the second (in both halves), or land in neither. The safe pattern is to force materialization (`WITH cte AS MATERIALIZED (...)` in Postgres 12+), or to compute the random value once into a real temp table. The same trap applies to NOW(), uuid_generate_v4(), and other volatile functions.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about NULL behavior in standard SQL are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)
options: `WHERE col != 'X'` excludes rows where col IS NULL
`SUM(col)` over an all-NULL or empty input returns NULL, not 0
`AVG(col)` divides by COUNT(col) (non-NULL), not COUNT(*)
`STRING_AGG(col, ',')` returns NULL for groups where every value is NULL
`MAX(col)` over an empty input returns 0
`COUNT(*)` counts NULL rows; `COUNT(col)` skips NULL values
questions.explanation: All five are correct except 'MAX over empty returns 0' — MAX over an empty (or all-NULL) input returns NULL. The others are the canonical NULL-handling rules: != filters drop NULLs (unless you OR `IS NULL`); SUM/AVG/STRING_AGG ignore NULLs; STRING_AGG over an all-NULL group returns NULL (not '' — you get NO output string, just NULL); COUNT(*) vs COUNT(col) differ by NULL handling. Master these and 80% of NULL bugs vanish.

questions.prompt: Which of the following silently converts a `LEFT JOIN` into an effective `INNER JOIN` (i.e. drops the unmatched left-side rows)? (Select all that apply.)
options: Adding `WHERE right.col = 'X'` after the LEFT JOIN
Adding `AND right.col = 'X'` inside the ON clause instead of WHERE
Adding `WHERE right.col IS NOT NULL` after the LEFT JOIN
Adding `WHERE right.col IS NULL` after the LEFT JOIN (this is the anti-join idiom)
Casting the left-side join key to a different type
Adding `GROUP BY left.id` to the outer query
questions.explanation: (0) Filtering on the right side in WHERE drops rows where right.col is NULL (because NULL = 'X' is NULL). Same with (2): explicitly requiring NOT NULL drops the unmatched rows. (1) is the CORRECT fix — a predicate in ON does not cause the collapse. (3) is the inverse — an anti-join, returns rows with NO match. (4) and (5) don't change LEFT vs INNER semantics.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are TRUE about default window-frame behavior in ANSI SQL when you write `func() OVER (ORDER BY x)` and OMIT an explicit frame clause? (Select all that apply.)
options: The default frame is `RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`
The default frame is `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`
With RANGE, rows tied on the ORDER BY value are treated as a single 'peer group' — they all see the same running aggregate
`LAST_VALUE()` with the default frame returns the value of the CURRENT row, not the partition's true last value
`LAG()` and `LEAD()` are affected by the default frame and may return tied peer values
If ORDER BY is omitted entirely, the default frame is `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING`
questions.explanation: ANSI's defaults: with ORDER BY, the frame is RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW; without ORDER BY (and for aggregate window functions), the frame becomes UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING. RANGE treats tied ORDER BY values as peers (this is THE running-total gotcha). LAST_VALUE with the default sliding frame returns the current row's value, not the true last — you must say `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING`. LAG/LEAD are positional and IGNORE the frame clause entirely — they're not affected by RANGE vs ROWS.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are realistic production bugs caused by integer / type-coercion quirks? (Select all that apply.)
options: `paid_count * 100 / total_count` returning 0 when both are integers and paid_count < total_count
`WHERE int_id = '42'` (string literal on integer column) defeating an index in some engines
`WHERE col = 0` matching rows where `col` is the string 'abc' in MySQL (default config)
`SUM(NULL + 1)` returning 1 because NULL is treated as 0
`SELECT CAST('2025-13-01' AS DATE)` silently returning NULL in some SQLite configurations
`'a' || NULL` returning 'a' in PostgreSQL
questions.explanation: (0) Integer division truncates: `5 * 100 / 10 = 50` but `5 / 10 * 100 = 0`. Force decimals: `paid_count * 100.0 / total_count`. (1) Implicit conversion on the column side can defeat an index. (2) MySQL coerces strings to numbers; 'abc' → 0. (4) SQLite's CAST is permissive and can return NULL for invalid dates rather than erroring. (3) is FALSE: NULL + 1 = NULL, and SUM of NULL inputs returns NULL (or 0 only when there's at least one non-NULL). (5) is FALSE: in Postgres, `'a' || NULL` returns NULL; Oracle is the engine that treats NULL as ''.

questions.prompt:
In PostgreSQL, you write:
```sql
UPDATE counters SET value = value + 1 WHERE id = 1;
```
Two concurrent transactions execute this on the same row at the same time, under the default READ COMMITTED isolation. What happens?
options: One transaction blocks waiting on the row lock; both succeed in sequence; final value is +2
Both transactions read `value` simultaneously and the second overwrites the first — the final value is only +1 (lost update)
PostgreSQL throws a serialization error because two writers touched the same row
Both transactions silently apply the +1, but the row is read-locked so the result is non-deterministic
questions.explanation: `UPDATE ... SET col = col + 1` is NOT a lost-update bug in Postgres because the UPDATE itself takes a row-level write lock and re-reads the current value INSIDE the lock. The second transaction blocks until the first commits, then reads the new value of `value` and adds 1. Final result: +2. The lost-update problem appears when you SELECT a value into the application, then UPDATE based on that stale read — not when the increment is done inside a single SQL statement. (Under REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE, behavior differs: Postgres will throw a serialization error or apply MVCC rules.)

questions.prompt:
Given (PostgreSQL):
```sql
UPDATE accounts a
SET balance = balance + d.amount
FROM deposits d
WHERE d.account_id = a.id;
```
Account 1 has TWO matching rows in `deposits`: amounts 100 and 50. After running, by how much has account 1's balance increased?
options: 150 — Postgres applies one update per matching row, summing both deposits
EITHER 100 OR 50 — when the FROM clause produces multiple matching rows per target row, Postgres applies the update only ONCE using ONE of the source rows, and which one is implementation-defined
0 — Postgres refuses to update on ambiguity
The query errors with 'multiple rows matched'
questions.explanation: Per the Postgres docs: 'When using FROM you should ensure that the join produces at most one output row for each row to be modified. If it does, then only ONE of the join rows will be used to update the target row, but which one will be used is not readily predictable.' Naive engineers expect `SET balance = balance + d.amount` to sum BOTH deposits (150) — it does NOT. Postgres applies a SINGLE update with ONE of the source rows; the other is silently discarded. The fix is to pre-aggregate: `FROM (SELECT account_id, SUM(amount) AS amt FROM deposits GROUP BY account_id) d`. SQL Server's `UPDATE ... FROM` behaves the same way (the 'Halloween' / multi-match update gotcha).

questions.prompt:
Schema: `users(id, country, opt_in)` where `opt_in` is one of 'yes', 'no', or NULL (never answered). Predict the output of the query below, then click Run to verify. Hint: think carefully about WHERE evaluating NULL.

```sql
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS c
FROM users
WHERE opt_in != 'yes'
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY country;
```

Your task: rewrite the query so the result INCLUDES users who never answered (opt_in IS NULL) alongside those who said 'no'. Same output columns `country, c` ordered by country.
questions.starter:
-- Rewrite to include NULL opt_in users alongside 'no' answers.
-- Output columns: country, c (count). Order by country.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS c
FROM users
WHERE opt_in IS DISTINCT FROM 'yes'
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY country;
key_points: Plain `opt_in != 'yes'` excludes NULLs (because NULL != 'yes' is NULL, not TRUE)
Fix is either `opt_in IS DISTINCT FROM 'yes'` (Postgres/SQLite) or `(opt_in != 'yes' OR opt_in IS NULL)`
Result must include the NULL responders — CA gets 2 (one 'no' + one NULL), FR gets 1 (NULL only), JP gets 2 (both 'no')

questions.prompt:
Schema: `orders(id, amt)` and `refunds(order_id, refund_amt)` (one order may have MANY refunds). A naive query `SELECT SUM(o.amt) FROM orders o LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.id = r.order_id` inflates the total because order rows are duplicated by the join. Run it once to confirm the inflated number, then write the CORRECT query that returns:

- `total_orders` — the genuine sum of `orders.amt`
- `total_refunds` — the genuine sum of `refunds.refund_amt`

BOTH columns in ONE row, with NO double-counting.
questions.starter:
-- Return one row: total_orders, total_refunds
-- Both numbers must NOT be inflated by the one-to-many join.
key_points: Aggregate each table separately — do NOT join then SUM
Equivalent approaches: scalar subqueries (shown), two CTEs cross-joined, or pre-aggregating the many-side before joining
The naive LEFT JOIN + SUM(o.amt) overcounts: order 1 fans out to 2 refund rows so its amt of 100 is counted twice (total 450 instead of 350)

questions.prompt:
Schema: `sales(d TEXT, amt INT)` where d is a date string. Multiple rows can share the same day. The query `SELECT d, amt, SUM(amt) OVER (ORDER BY d) FROM sales` gives a SURPRISING result because the default window frame is RANGE — tied rows on the same `d` are all assigned the same running total (the lump).

Write the CORRECT query that produces a strict, deterministic per-row running total. Return columns `d, amt, running_total` ordered by (d, amt).
questions.starter:
-- Return d, amt, running_total
-- running_total must accumulate row by row (no lumping on tied days)
questions.model_answer:
SELECT d, amt,
       SUM(amt) OVER (ORDER BY d, amt ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total
FROM sales
ORDER BY d, amt;
key_points: Use ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW — not the default RANGE
Add a tie-breaker to ORDER BY (e.g. amt) so the row order is deterministic
Without the explicit ROWS frame, both Jan-01 rows would share running_total = 150

questions.prompt: You're emulating an ASOF JOIN in standard SQL (no native ASOF) on a 1-billion-row `trades` table joined to a 10-million-row `quotes` table. All four options below are functionally correct. Which is the MOST efficient at this scale?
options: Correlated subquery — `(SELECT bid FROM quotes WHERE symbol = t.symbol AND ts <= t.ts ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1)` for each trade row
LATERAL JOIN with `ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1` — runs the inner query once per trade
Window-function fill — UNION ALL trades + quotes on a unified timestamp, then `LAST_VALUE(bid) IGNORE NULLS` over the interleaved timeline
Inequality JOIN + GROUP BY MAX(quote_ts), then JOIN back to quotes to retrieve the bid
questions.explanation:
All four are functionally correct, but the cost models diverge sharply at scale.

- **Correlated subquery / LATERAL** — O(N · log M). One index probe per left row. Fine at 100K rows, fatal at 1B (a billion index lookups dominates the wall clock).
- **Inequality JOIN + GROUP BY MAX** — materializes a near-Cartesian intermediate before MAX-collapses it. Spills to disk; memory blows up.
- **Window-function fill** — single linear pass after one sort. O(N log N) total, no per-row probing. The interleaved-timeline pattern is essentially what ClickHouse / DuckDB do internally for ASOF JOIN.

This is also why ClickHouse / DuckDB / kdb+ bothered to add native ASOF: at finance / telemetry scale, the SQL emulations either die (correlated) or require careful rewriting (window fill). The native operator hides the complexity and adds specialized index structures (sort-merge, B-tree on (key, ts)) that beat O(N log N).

questions.prompt:
Emulate an ASOF JOIN in Postgres without using `IGNORE NULLS` (so this technique works in any SQL with window functions — Postgres < 14, older BigQuery, MySQL 8). Schema:

- `quotes(ts, symbol, bid)` — bid updates per symbol
- `trades(ts, symbol, qty)` — trades per symbol

For each trade, return the most recent bid AT-OR-BEFORE the trade time, per symbol. Use the window-function-fill pattern with the gap-fill trick. Return columns: `trade_ts, symbol, qty, active_bid`. Order by `symbol, trade_ts`.

Hint: interleave both tables with UNION ALL, then use `COUNT(bid) OVER (... ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING)` to define fill-forward groups, then `MAX(bid) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol, grp)`. Compute the MAX BEFORE filtering to trade rows — otherwise WHERE strips the quote rows first and MAX has no bids to find.
questions.starter:
-- Your SQL here
-- Pattern:
--   timeline CTE (UNION ALL of quotes + trades)
--   fill CTE (group via COUNT)
--   with_active CTE (MAX over group — DO THIS BEFORE WHERE)
--   final SELECT (filter to trade rows)
questions.model_answer:
WITH timeline AS (
  SELECT ts, symbol, CAST(NULL AS REAL) AS qty, bid FROM quotes
  UNION ALL
  SELECT ts, symbol, qty, CAST(NULL AS INTEGER) AS bid FROM trades
),
fill AS (
  SELECT ts, symbol, qty, bid,
         COUNT(bid) OVER (
           PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY ts
           ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
         ) AS grp
  FROM timeline
),
with_active AS (
  SELECT ts, symbol, qty,
         MAX(bid) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol, grp) AS active_bid
  FROM fill
)
SELECT ts AS trade_ts, symbol, qty, active_bid
FROM with_active
WHERE qty IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY symbol, trade_ts;
key_points: Interleaves the two tables on a unified timestamp via UNION ALL
Uses COUNT(bid) OVER (ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) to define gap-fill groups — increments only when a non-null bid is seen
Computes MAX(bid) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol, grp) in a SEPARATE CTE before the WHERE filter — otherwise WHERE removes the quote rows first and MAX has no bids in-scope
Filters to trade rows (`WHERE qty IS NOT NULL`) at the end
PARTITION BY symbol so symbols don't bleed into each other

questions.prompt: Uber needs to attach the active surge multiplier to each ride request. Surge updates every minute via a `surge(ts, city_id, multiplier)` stream; ride requests arrive continuously. A user requests at 12:05:10; the most recent surge was at 12:04:00 (1.5x). Which approach is most appropriate for the REAL-TIME pricing path (sub-second latency)?
options: Run a correlated subquery against the warehouse for each request
Run the window-function-fill SQL pattern on every request as it arrives
Maintain the latest surge per city in Redis (or a Flink `MapState` keyed by city_id); each request does a single GET
Wait for the next batch ETL job to align requests with surge offline
questions.explanation:
For sub-second pricing, neither warehouse-style SQL pattern fits — they're milliseconds-to-seconds, not microseconds. The real-time pattern is:

1. **Keyed state lookup**: surge updates are written to Redis (or a Flink `MapState` keyed by `city_id`). Each request does ONE `GET city_id` and gets the current multiplier. Latency: sub-millisecond.
2. **Flink temporal table join** does the same declaratively — surge is registered as a versioned table, the request stream joins against the version active at request time, and Flink keeps only the latest version per key in RocksDB.

The SQL patterns (correlated subquery, window-fill) become correct for the BATCH path later — analytics, payouts, fraud review — when latency tolerance is minutes, not milliseconds. The batch path uses the LANDED surge history and an ASOF JOIN (or the emulation). Option 4 fails the freshness SLA for in-the-moment pricing.

The pattern: **state lives in fast key-value when freshness matters; in the warehouse for correctness over history.**

questions.prompt: Netflix joins `playback_events(user_id, ts, video_id)` to `experiment_history(user_id, bucket, valid_from, valid_to)` to determine which A/B bucket a user was in WHEN they played a video. A user can move between buckets over time, so each user has multiple history rows. `valid_to` is `NULL` if the bucket is still active. Which join correctly attaches the bucket that was active at play time?
options: `JOIN experiment_history e ON p.user_id = e.user_id` — pick any matching row
`JOIN experiment_history e ON p.user_id = e.user_id AND e.valid_to IS NULL`
`JOIN experiment_history e ON p.user_id = e.user_id AND p.ts >= e.valid_from AND (p.ts < e.valid_to OR e.valid_to IS NULL)`
`LEFT JOIN experiment_history e ON p.user_id = e.user_id WHERE p.ts BETWEEN e.valid_from AND e.valid_to`
questions.explanation:
The correct approach is a **range join**: anchor on `user_id`, then bound the play time within the history row's validity interval, treating `valid_to IS NULL` as 'open-ended / currently active'.

- **Option 1** fans out: every play gets every history row the user ever had — analytics gets multiplied by bucket-history length.
- **Option 2** is the 'current bucket' join — wrong for historical playbacks that happened BEFORE the current bucket started. Backfill / model training needs the bucket that was active *at the play moment*, not now.
- **Option 3** is correct. Note the **half-open interval** (`<`, not `<=`) on `valid_to` so the bucket-change boundary belongs to exactly one row.
- **Option 4** uses `BETWEEN`, which is closed on both ends AND fails on `valid_to IS NULL` (BETWEEN against NULL is NULL — the row silently drops from the result).

This is exactly the case ASOF JOIN exists to handle. In ClickHouse you'd write `ASOF JOIN experiment_history ON user_id AND ts >= valid_from`. In standard SQL, the range join with the NULL-aware upper bound is the answer.

questions.prompt:
Schema preloaded: `price_logs(event_time, price)` with two rows sharing `12:05:00`. Write a SELECT that — for each row — returns the `SUM` and the `STRING_AGG` **formula** (e.g. `'10 + 20'`) for BOTH `ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` and `GROUPS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`, so you can SEE exactly which prices each frame is adding.

Return columns: `event_time, price, rows_sum, rows_formula, groups_sum, groups_formula` ordered by `event_time, price`. Cast `price` to `text` for STRING_AGG (Postgres requires explicit types).
questions.starter: -- Two SUMs and two STRING_AGGs with the same frames
questions.model_answer:
SELECT event_time, price,
  SUM(price)                        OVER (ORDER BY event_time ROWS   BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS rows_sum,
  STRING_AGG(price::text, ' + ')    OVER (ORDER BY event_time ROWS   BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS rows_formula,
  SUM(price)                        OVER (ORDER BY event_time GROUPS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS groups_sum,
  STRING_AGG(price::text, ' + ')    OVER (ORDER BY event_time GROUPS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS groups_formula
FROM price_logs
ORDER BY event_time, price;
key_points: Uses STRING_AGG as a WINDOW function — most engineers only learn it as a GROUP BY aggregate
Same frame clause repeated for SUM and STRING_AGG so the formula matches the math
Cast `price::text` because STRING_AGG expects text input in Postgres
ORDER BY event_time inside the OVER() — the frame requires a sort order to be defined
Reveals that both 12:05 rows have IDENTICAL groups_sum (50) — proof that GROUPS treats peers as one unit; their rows_sum (30 vs 40) diverges because ROWS counts physical rows

questions.prompt:
You write the following expecting `final_status` to be the user's LAST-ever status for each user:

```sql
SELECT user_id, ts, status,
  LAST_VALUE(status) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS final_status
FROM events;
```

What do you actually get, and why?
options: The user's last-ever status — `LAST_VALUE` is designed to do exactly that.
NULL for every row, because `LAST_VALUE` requires a frame to be explicit.

Each row's `final_status` equals its OWN `status` (or the status of the last row in its peer group). Reason: when you include `ORDER BY` without an explicit frame, the ANSI default frame is `RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` — and 'CURRENT ROW' in `RANGE`/`GROUPS` means 'end of current peer group'. So `LAST_VALUE` returns the last value WITHIN that bounded frame, not the partition's true last value.
An error, because `LAST_VALUE` and `PARTITION BY` are mutually exclusive.
questions.explanation:
This is **the #1 production bug** in window-function code — engineers expect `LAST_VALUE` to 'just work' like `FIRST_VALUE` appears to.

The ANSI default frame when `ORDER BY` is present is `RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`. `LAST_VALUE` evaluates the last value INSIDE that frame, which is the current row (or, for tied rows, the last row of their peer group) — almost never what the engineer wanted.

**The two correct fixes:**

1. **Explicit unbounded frame:**
```sql
LAST_VALUE(status) OVER (
  PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts
  ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
) AS final_status
```

2. **Flip to FIRST_VALUE with reverse order** (often clearer):
```sql
FIRST_VALUE(status) OVER (
  PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts DESC
) AS final_status
```

The FIRST_VALUE flip works because the default frame's 'last row of CURRENT ROW peer group' includes the partition's *first* row when there's no preceding data — so `FIRST_VALUE` happens to return what you want; `LAST_VALUE` does not.

**Why this misconception persists**: every textbook example shows `FIRST_VALUE` and `LAST_VALUE` together as if they're symmetric. They are NOT symmetric under the default frame. Always set the frame explicitly when using `LAST_VALUE`.

questions.prompt:
Why does this SQL throw a syntax / semantic error?

```sql
SELECT user_id, ts,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    ORDER BY ts
    ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS rn
FROM events;
```
options: `ROW_NUMBER` is a reserved keyword in some dialects

**Window functions come in two classes, and only one accepts a frame clause.** Ranking functions — `ROW_NUMBER`, `RANK`, `DENSE_RANK`, `NTILE`, `PERCENT_RANK`, `CUME_DIST` — and offset functions `LAG`/`LEAD` are position-based: by definition they look at the entire partition, so a frame is meaningless and the parser rejects it. Only AGGREGATE-as-window functions (`SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `FIRST_VALUE`, `LAST_VALUE`, `NTH_VALUE`, `ARRAY_AGG`, `STRING_AGG`, …) accept `ROWS` / `GROUPS` / `RANGE` frames.
The frame should be `RANGE` instead of `ROWS`
You need `PARTITION BY` for window functions to compile
questions.explanation:
Two classes of window functions:

**Class 1 — Ranking & offset (NO frame allowed):**
- `ROW_NUMBER()`, `RANK()`, `DENSE_RANK()`, `NTILE(n)`, `PERCENT_RANK()`, `CUME_DIST()` — assign a positional label to every row in the partition
- `LAG(col [, n [, default]])`, `LEAD(col [, n [, default]])` — look at a row at a fixed offset; the offset is the argument, not a frame

**Class 2 — Aggregate-as-window (frame REQUIRED for correct semantics, default = RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW):**
- `SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`
- `FIRST_VALUE`, `LAST_VALUE`, `NTH_VALUE`
- `STRING_AGG` / `LISTAGG` / `GROUP_CONCAT`, `ARRAY_AGG`

**What you probably meant:**
- 'count rows in a sliding 2-row window' → `COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)`
- 'get position-in-partition with no sliding window' → `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY ts)` (no frame)
- 'previous row's value' → `LAG(col) OVER (ORDER BY ts)`

Distractor option 3 (PARTITION BY) is a red herring: `OVER (ORDER BY ts)` without `PARTITION BY` is valid — the partition is the entire query.

questions.prompt: Schema: `quotes(ts TIMESTAMP, bid INT)`. You want a 5-minute-trailing average of `bid` — for each quote, the average of all bids whose timestamp is within the previous 5 minutes (inclusive). Which window frame correctly expresses this?
options: `ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN 5 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`
`ORDER BY ts GROUPS BETWEEN 5 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`
`ORDER BY ts RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '5 minutes' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`
`ORDER BY ts RANGE BETWEEN 5 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`
questions.explanation:
Three frame modes, three different ways of counting `5 PRECEDING`:

- **`ROWS BETWEEN 5 PRECEDING …`** — five physical rows back. If quotes arrive 1 per second, this is the last 5 seconds. If they arrive 1 per hour, this is the last 5 hours. Wrong unit.
- **`GROUPS BETWEEN 5 PRECEDING …`** — five distinct timestamp values back. If timestamps are unique (no ties), this also walks 5 rows back — but the unit is 'distinct value', not 'minute'. Still wrong.
- **`RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '5 minutes' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`** — correct: RANGE measures using the ORDER BY column's TYPE-AWARE arithmetic. With a timestamp ORDER BY, the offset is a time interval. The frame is 'all rows whose `ts >= current_ts − 5 minutes`'.
- **`RANGE BETWEEN 5 PRECEDING …`** without an interval — works only when the ORDER BY column is numeric. For a TIMESTAMP column, this is a type error.

**This is the killer feature of RANGE**: it understands the TYPE of the ORDER BY column. For numeric columns you can say `RANGE BETWEEN 100 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`. For timestamps, `INTERVAL '5 minutes'`. For dates, `INTERVAL '7 days'`. The semantics are 'value-aware', not 'position-aware'.

Production usage:
- Streaming dashboards (last-N-minute moving averages)
- Anomaly detection (z-score against trailing 30-day window)
- Cohort retention (signups within 7 days of campaign launch)

Supported: Postgres, Oracle, Snowflake, BigQuery (with caveats), DuckDB. NOT in SQLite, NOT in MySQL ≤ 8.0.

questions.prompt: Schema: `sales(customer_id, order_date, product_id, qty)` and `menu(product_id, name, category, price)`. For each customer, return the FIRST product they purchased in EACH category, where 'first' is by order_date ascending; ties on date are broken by lower price, then by product_id ascending. Output columns: `customer_id, category, product_name, order_date`, ordered by customer_id, category. A customer who never bought in a category should produce NO row for that category.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT customer_id, category, name AS product_name, order_date
FROM (
  SELECT s.customer_id, m.category, m.name, s.order_date,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY s.customer_id, m.category
                            ORDER BY s.order_date, m.price, m.product_id) AS rn
  FROM sales s JOIN menu m USING (product_id)
) t
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY customer_id, category;
key_points: ROW_NUMBER partitioned by (customer_id, category)
Multi-key ORDER BY for deterministic tie-break (date, then price, then id)
Filter rn=1 in outer query — windows can't sit in WHERE
Customer C bought only 'main' — no dessert/drink rows are emitted

questions.prompt: Schema: `members(customer_id, join_date)` and `purchases(customer_id, order_date, product, amount)`. Compute loyalty points per customer for January 2025 using these rules: (a) every dollar = 10 points base; (b) within the FIRST 7 DAYS after join_date (inclusive of join_date itself), every dollar = 20 points (1st-week bonus); (c) on any 'sushi' purchase OUTSIDE the first-week bonus, every dollar = 20 points. Non-members contribute 10 points/dollar with no bonuses. Return columns `customer_id, points`, ordered by customer_id. Only consider purchases in January 2025.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE members (customer_id TEXT, join_date DATE);
INSERT INTO members VALUES ('A','2021-01-07'),('B','2021-01-09');
CREATE TABLE purchases (customer_id TEXT, order_date DATE, product TEXT, amount INTEGER);
INSERT INTO purchases VALUES
  ('A','2021-01-01','sushi',10),
  ('A','2021-01-01','curry',15),
  ('A','2021-01-07','curry',15),
  ('A','2021-01-10','ramen',12),
  ('A','2021-01-11','ramen',12),
  ('A','2021-01-11','ramen',12),
  ('B','2021-01-01','curry',15),
  ('B','2021-01-02','curry',15),
  ('B','2021-01-04','sushi',10),
  ('B','2021-01-11','sushi',10),
  ('B','2021-01-16','ramen',12),
  ('C','2021-01-01','ramen',12),
  ('C','2021-01-07','ramen',12);
questions.model_answer:
SELECT p.customer_id,
       SUM(
         CASE
           WHEN m.join_date IS NOT NULL
                AND p.order_date BETWEEN m.join_date AND m.join_date + 6
             THEN p.amount * 20
           WHEN p.product = 'sushi' THEN p.amount * 20
           ELSE p.amount * 10
         END
       ) AS points
FROM purchases p
LEFT JOIN members m ON m.customer_id = p.customer_id
WHERE p.order_date >= DATE '2021-01-01'
  AND p.order_date <  DATE '2021-02-01'
GROUP BY p.customer_id
ORDER BY p.customer_id;
key_points: CASE chain inside SUM: first-week bonus checked BEFORE sushi rule (priority)
LEFT JOIN to members so non-members still appear, with NULL join_date → falls to ELSE
Sargable date filter (>=, <) instead of EXTRACT(MONTH FROM ...)
INTERVAL via `join_date + 6` (integer days) — Postgres supports date + int

questions.prompt: Schema: `users(user_id, signup_date)` and `activity(user_id, active_date)`. For each WEEKLY cohort (week start = Monday of the signup date), report retention by week offset 0..3, where week offset N means 'was the user active during the 7-day window starting cohort_week_start + 7*N'. Return columns `cohort_week, week_offset, retained_users`. Ordering: cohort_week ASC, week_offset ASC. Include offset rows only if at least one user was retained (skip 0-counts).
questions.model_answer:
WITH cohorts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         date_trunc('week', signup_date)::date AS cohort_week
  FROM users
),
joined AS (
  SELECT c.cohort_week,
         c.user_id,
         FLOOR((a.active_date - c.cohort_week)::numeric / 7)::int AS week_offset
  FROM cohorts c
  JOIN activity a USING (user_id)
)
SELECT cohort_week,
       week_offset,
       COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS retained_users
FROM joined
WHERE week_offset BETWEEN 0 AND 3
GROUP BY cohort_week, week_offset
ORDER BY cohort_week, week_offset;
key_points: date_trunc('week', d) snaps to Monday in Postgres (ISO weeks)
Week offset = floor((active_date - cohort_week)/7) — integer division avoided via numeric
COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) so a user active twice in the same week counts once
GROUP BY drops zero-retention cells naturally — they never appear in `joined`

questions.prompt: Schema: `pizzas(pizza_id, name)` and `orders(order_id, customer_id, pizza_id)`. Find customers who have ordered AT LEAST ONE of EVERY pizza in the menu. Return `customer_id`, ordered ascending. Edge case: if a customer ordered the same pizza multiple times, that counts as one. If the menu is empty (no pizzas), every customer trivially qualifies.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT customer_id
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT pizza_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pizzas)
ORDER BY customer_id;
key_points: Relational division via COUNT(DISTINCT) = total menu size
Customer 2 has only 2/3 pizzas — excluded; Customer 4 has only 1 — excluded
Customers 1 and 3 both qualify (1's repeat of Margherita is collapsed by DISTINCT)
Alternative: NOT EXISTS pattern (no pizza missing from the customer's set)

questions.prompt: Schema: `purchases(customer_id, product)`. Return customers who have purchased AT LEAST ONE product from the set {'A','B'} but NEVER purchased any product from {'X','Y','Z'}. Return `customer_id`, ordered ascending.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE purchases (customer_id INTEGER, product TEXT);
INSERT INTO purchases VALUES
  (1,'A'),(1,'B'),(1,'C'),
  (2,'A'),(2,'X'),
  (3,'B'),(3,'C'),
  (4,'B'),(4,'D'),(4,'E'),
  (5,'C'),(5,'D'),
  (6,'A'),(6,'Y'),
  (7,'B');
questions.model_answer:
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id
FROM purchases
WHERE product IN ('A','B')
  AND customer_id NOT IN (
        SELECT customer_id FROM purchases WHERE product IN ('X','Y','Z')
      )
ORDER BY customer_id;
key_points: Two-sided membership test: positive (IN) and negative (NOT IN sub-select)
DISTINCT because a customer with both A and B would appear twice otherwise
Customer 2 has A but also X — excluded; Customer 6 has A but Y — excluded; Customer 5 has neither A nor B — excluded
NULL-safe? Yes here because product is NOT NULL in the schema — otherwise use NOT EXISTS

questions.prompt: Schema: `txn(id, account, ts, kind, amount)` where `kind` is 'deposit' (positive contribution), 'withdrawal' (negative), or 'refund' (positive — credit applied to the account). Compute the running balance per account ordered by ts. Return `id, account, ts, kind, signed_amount, running_balance`, ordered by account, ts, id. `signed_amount` is the per-row delta (negative for withdrawals); `running_balance` is the cumulative.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT id, account, ts, kind,
       CASE WHEN kind = 'withdrawal' THEN -amount ELSE amount END AS signed_amount,
       SUM(CASE WHEN kind = 'withdrawal' THEN -amount ELSE amount END)
         OVER (PARTITION BY account ORDER BY ts, id
               ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_balance
FROM txn
ORDER BY account, ts, id;
key_points: Per-row sign mapping via CASE before aggregation
Cumulative SUM over window partitioned by account, ordered by ts + tiebreaker id
Explicit ROWS frame to avoid the RANGE default issue with same-ts rows
Refunds add to balance just like deposits in this schema; withdrawals subtract

questions.prompt: Schema: `sensor_readings(ts, sensor_id, value)` where `value` may be NULL for sensor downtime. For each (ts, sensor_id) row, return the LAST KNOWN non-NULL value (LOCF, last observation carried forward) for that sensor. If no prior non-NULL exists, return NULL. Implement WITHOUT using `IGNORE NULLS`. Return `ts, sensor_id, value, filled` ordered by sensor_id, ts.
questions.model_answer:
WITH grouped AS (
  SELECT ts, sensor_id, value,
         COUNT(value) OVER (PARTITION BY sensor_id ORDER BY ts
                            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS grp
  FROM sensor_readings
)
SELECT ts, sensor_id, value,
       MAX(value) OVER (PARTITION BY sensor_id, grp) AS filled
FROM grouped
ORDER BY sensor_id, ts;
key_points: Two-pass trick: COUNT(value) jumps on every non-NULL, creating monotone groups
Within each group, the single non-NULL is the MAX/MIN — propagated across the group
Works on every engine — no IGNORE NULLS required
Sensor 2 starts with NULLs — no prior non-NULL exists, so `filled` stays NULL until 02:00

questions.prompt: Schema: `products(category, name, sales)`. For each category, return the TOP 3 products by sales DESC, but INCLUDING TIES at the boundary — so if two products tie for 3rd place, both are returned (4 rows for that category). Return `category, name, sales`, ordered by category, sales DESC, name ASC.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT category, name, sales
FROM (
  SELECT category, name, sales,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) AS rk
  FROM products
) t
WHERE rk <= 3
ORDER BY category, sales DESC, name;
key_points: DENSE_RANK (not ROW_NUMBER) — DENSE_RANK gives same rank to ties so we include them
Category A: rk 1,2,3,3 — ties for 3rd both retained → 4 rows
Category B: every product tied at rank 1 — all 3 included; rank 2 is p9
Category C: only one product — returned as-is

questions.prompt: Schema: `subs(customer_id, start_date, plan)` is an append-only log of subscription state changes (one row per change). Compute the total number of DAYS each customer spent in each plan up to a reporting cutoff of 2025-12-31. End of a plan period = start_date of the customer's next row (exclusive), or the cutoff for the last row. Return `customer_id, plan, days`, ordered by customer_id, plan.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE subs (customer_id INTEGER, start_date DATE, plan TEXT);
INSERT INTO subs VALUES
  (1,'2025-01-01','trial'),
  (1,'2025-01-08','basic'),
  (1,'2025-02-15','pro'),
  (1,'2025-12-01','churn'),
  (2,'2025-03-10','trial'),
  (2,'2025-03-17','annual');
questions.model_answer:
WITH intervals AS (
  SELECT customer_id, plan, start_date,
         COALESCE(
           LEAD(start_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY start_date),
           DATE '2026-01-01'
         ) AS end_exclusive
  FROM subs
)
SELECT customer_id, plan,
       SUM((LEAST(end_exclusive, DATE '2026-01-01') - start_date))::int AS days
FROM intervals
WHERE start_date < DATE '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY customer_id, plan
ORDER BY customer_id, plan;
key_points: LEAD to get the next change-time; COALESCE the final row to the cutoff
Half-open interval: [start, end_exclusive) — date subtraction yields integer days in Postgres
LEAST(end_exclusive, cutoff) clips a still-active plan to the reporting window
GROUP BY (customer_id, plan) sums multiple stints in the same plan if they ever happened

questions.prompt: Schema: `employee(id, name, manager_id)` where the CEO has manager_id = NULL. For each employee, return the path from CEO down to them, as a `>`-separated string of names, plus the depth (CEO is 0). Return `id, name, depth, path`, ordered by depth then id.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (id INTEGER, name TEXT, manager_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),(3,'Cara',1),
  (4,'Dan',2),(5,'Eve',2),
  (6,'Finn',3),
  (7,'Gwen',4);
questions.model_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE chain AS (
  SELECT id, name, 0 AS depth, name AS path
  FROM employee
  WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, c.depth + 1, c.path || ' > ' || e.name
  FROM employee e
  JOIN chain c ON e.manager_id = c.id
)
SELECT id, name, depth, path
FROM chain
ORDER BY depth, id;
key_points: Anchor seeds with the CEO (manager_id IS NULL)
Recursive step accumulates the path string and increments depth
UNION ALL — no need to dedup in a strict tree
Path printing makes the tree shape obvious for debugging

questions.prompt: Schema: `score(category, value)`. Compute the MEDIAN value per category WITHOUT using PERCENTILE_CONT, PERCENTILE_DISC, or any percentile/quantile function. Use ROW_NUMBER + COUNT and average the middle row(s). Median definition: for odd N return the middle value; for even N return the average of the two middle values. Return `category, median`, ordered by category. Round to 2 decimals.
questions.model_answer:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT category, value,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY value) AS rn,
         COUNT(*)    OVER (PARTITION BY category)                  AS n
  FROM score
)
SELECT category,
       ROUND(AVG(value)::numeric, 2) AS median
FROM ranked
WHERE rn IN ((n + 1) / 2, (n + 2) / 2)   -- integer arithmetic handles odd & even
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;
key_points: ROW_NUMBER over (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY value) gives positional ranking
Window-COUNT to know n inside the same pass
Odd n: (n+1)/2 = (n+2)/2 → one middle row; Even n: two middle rows averaged
Avoids PERCENTILE_CONT — useful when targeting engines that lack it

questions.prompt: Schema: `status_log(entity_id, ts, status)`. Compress consecutive identical statuses per entity into stints. For each (entity_id, run of same status), return the entity, status, stint_start (first ts), stint_end (last ts), and row_count. Return `entity_id, stint_start, stint_end, status, row_count`, ordered by entity_id then stint_start.
questions.model_answer:
WITH flagged AS (
  SELECT entity_id, ts, status,
         LAG(status) OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY ts) AS prev_status
  FROM status_log
),
changes AS (
  SELECT entity_id, ts, status,
         SUM(CASE WHEN prev_status IS DISTINCT FROM status THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
           OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY ts
                 ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS grp
  FROM flagged
)
SELECT entity_id,
       MIN(ts) AS stint_start,
       MAX(ts) AS stint_end,
       MIN(status) AS status,
       COUNT(*) AS row_count
FROM changes
GROUP BY entity_id, grp
ORDER BY entity_id, stint_start;
key_points: Classic gaps-and-islands: LAG to detect transitions, then cumulative SUM of the transition flag
IS DISTINCT FROM is NULL-safe — handles the first row (prev_status IS NULL) cleanly
Group by (entity_id, grp) collapses every consecutive run into one row
Same `open` appears twice for entity 1 — correctly as two separate stints

questions.prompt: Schema: three tables — `orders(order_id, customer_id, amount)`, `payments(order_id, paid_amount)`, `refunds(order_id, refund_amount)`. An order may have 0..N payments and 0..N refunds; the same order can have multiple of each row. Compute per-order: order_amount, total_paid, total_refunded, net (paid − refunded). Return EVERY order (even with no payments or refunds), with NULLs replaced by 0. Flag `status`: 'overpaid' if total_paid > order_amount, 'underpaid' if total_paid < order_amount, 'paid' if equal AND refunds = 0, 'refunded' if equal AND refunds > 0. Columns: `order_id, order_amount, total_paid, total_refunded, net, status`, ordered by order_id.
questions.model_answer:
WITH p AS (
  SELECT order_id, COALESCE(SUM(paid_amount),0) AS total_paid
  FROM payments GROUP BY order_id
),
r AS (
  SELECT order_id, COALESCE(SUM(refund_amount),0) AS total_refunded
  FROM refunds  GROUP BY order_id
)
SELECT o.order_id,
       o.amount AS order_amount,
       COALESCE(p.total_paid, 0)      AS total_paid,
       COALESCE(r.total_refunded, 0)  AS total_refunded,
       COALESCE(p.total_paid, 0) - COALESCE(r.total_refunded, 0) AS net,
       CASE
         WHEN COALESCE(p.total_paid,0) > o.amount THEN 'overpaid'
         WHEN COALESCE(p.total_paid,0) < o.amount THEN 'underpaid'
         WHEN COALESCE(r.total_refunded,0) > 0    THEN 'refunded'
         ELSE 'paid'
       END AS status
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN p USING (order_id)
LEFT JOIN r USING (order_id)
ORDER BY o.order_id;
key_points: Pre-aggregate each one-to-many child to one row per order — avoids the fan-out / Cartesian explosion
LEFT JOIN preserves orders with no payments/refunds; COALESCE turns NULLs to 0
Status CASE has correct precedence: overpaid/underpaid checked before equal+refunds
Order 4 has no payments → underpaid; Order 5 fully paid but refunded → refunded

questions.prompt: Schema: `txn(customer_id, category, ts, amount)`. For each (customer, category) row, compute the rolling average of the customer's LAST 3 transactions IN THAT CATEGORY (including the current row). If fewer than 3 prior rows exist, average over what's available. Return `customer_id, category, ts, amount, rolling_avg_3`, rounded to 2 decimals, ordered by customer_id, category, ts.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT customer_id, category, ts, amount,
       ROUND(AVG(amount) OVER (
         PARTITION BY customer_id, category
         ORDER BY ts
         ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       )::numeric, 2) AS rolling_avg_3
FROM txn
ORDER BY customer_id, category, ts;
key_points: Window partitioned by (customer, category) — each combo has its own series
ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW = 3-row trailing window
AVG handles partial windows automatically — first row is its own average
ROWS (not RANGE) avoids the same-ts peer-collapse trap

questions.prompt: Schema: `event(user_id, event, ts)` with possible events 'visit', 'add_to_cart', 'purchase'. Find users who completed the funnel IN ORDER: first visit, then add_to_cart, then purchase, where each step's earliest occurrence is strictly later than the prior step's earliest occurrence. Return `user_id`, ordered ascending.
questions.model_answer:
WITH firsts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event = 'visit')       AS first_visit,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event = 'add_to_cart') AS first_cart,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event = 'purchase')    AS first_purchase
  FROM event GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT user_id FROM firsts
WHERE first_visit IS NOT NULL
  AND first_cart IS NOT NULL
  AND first_purchase IS NOT NULL
  AND first_visit < first_cart
  AND first_cart  < first_purchase
ORDER BY user_id;
key_points: FILTER (WHERE event = ...) — Postgres-native conditional aggregation
Pivot first-time-of-each-event into one wide row per user — much simpler than self-joins
Strict ordering: first_visit < first_cart < first_purchase
User 2 purchased before adding to cart → excluded; user 4 added to cart before visiting → excluded

questions.prompt: Schema: `inventory_event(warehouse_id, sku, ts, delta)` where delta is positive for receipts and negative for shipments. For each (warehouse, sku, ts) row, compute the running on-hand quantity after that event. Return `warehouse_id, sku, ts, delta, on_hand`, ordered by warehouse_id, sku, ts.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT warehouse_id, sku, ts, delta,
       SUM(delta) OVER (PARTITION BY warehouse_id, sku ORDER BY ts
                        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS on_hand
FROM inventory_event
ORDER BY warehouse_id, sku, ts;
key_points: Cumulative SUM per (warehouse, sku) — separate ledger per location
Explicit ROWS frame avoids the default-RANGE peer-collapse trap on duplicate ts
Negative deltas reduce on_hand — same window function handles both
ORDER BY ts is the temporal dimension; the partition key is the inventory identity

questions.prompt: Schema: `orders(order_id, customer_id, category, ts, amount)`. For each (customer, category), return the SECOND-most-recent order — i.e. the order with the second-largest ts in that group. If a customer has only one order in a category, that category should NOT appear for that customer. Return `customer_id, category, order_id, ts, amount`, ordered by customer_id, category.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT customer_id, category, order_id, ts, amount
FROM (
  SELECT customer_id, category, order_id, ts, amount,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id, category ORDER BY ts DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders
) t
WHERE rn = 2
ORDER BY customer_id, category;
key_points: ROW_NUMBER ordered ts DESC; second-most-recent has rn=2
Customer 1 Y category has only one order — rn=2 doesn't exist → row absent (correct)
Customer 3 has only one X order — also excluded
Use ROW_NUMBER (not RANK) — ties on ts would otherwise return multiple 'second' rows

questions.prompt: Schema: `subs(customer_id, start_date, plan)` is an append-only log of plan changes. Count how many customers transitioned from 'trial' to each subsequent plan (including 'churn' or never-converted). Output one row per next_plan value (including the row 'never_converted' for customers whose trial had no next plan). Return `next_plan, customer_count`, ordered by customer_count DESC then next_plan ASC.
questions.model_answer:
WITH journeys AS (
  SELECT customer_id, plan,
         LEAD(plan) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY start_date) AS next_plan
  FROM subs
)
SELECT COALESCE(next_plan, 'never_converted') AS next_plan,
       COUNT(*) AS customer_count
FROM journeys
WHERE plan = 'trial'
GROUP BY COALESCE(next_plan, 'never_converted')
ORDER BY customer_count DESC, next_plan;
key_points: LEAD over (customer_id ORDER BY start_date) fetches the next plan in the journey
Filter to trial rows only — we want what came after trial
COALESCE the NULL LEAD result into 'never_converted' so the missing transition is counted
Reusable pattern for any 'state X → state Y' transition counting

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer_orders(order_id, pizza_id, order_time)` and `runner_orders(order_id, pickup_time, cancellation)`. Each order may contain several pizza rows (one per pizza). For each order, compute its size (count of pizza rows) and preparation time in minutes = pickup_time − MIN(order_time) for that order. Then, for each distinct pizza-count, report the AVERAGE preparation time rounded to 2 decimals. Exclude cancellations (cancellation is non-NULL and non-empty). Return `pizzas_in_order, avg_prep_minutes`, ordered by pizzas_in_order.
questions.model_answer:
WITH per_order AS (
  SELECT co.order_id,
         COUNT(*) AS pizzas_in_order,
         EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (ro.pickup_time - MIN(co.order_time)))/60.0 AS prep_minutes
  FROM customer_orders co
  JOIN runner_orders ro USING (order_id)
  WHERE ro.cancellation IS NULL OR ro.cancellation = ''
  GROUP BY co.order_id, ro.pickup_time
)
SELECT pizzas_in_order,
       ROUND(AVG(prep_minutes)::numeric, 2) AS avg_prep_minutes
FROM per_order
GROUP BY pizzas_in_order
ORDER BY pizzas_in_order;
key_points: Two-stage aggregation: per-order first, then per-pizza-count
EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (a - b))/60 — Postgres idiom for minute differences
Filter out cancellations early to avoid NULL prep times
MIN(order_time) per order handles the duplicated order_time across pizza rows

questions.prompt: Schema: `friendship(a, b)` of UNDIRECTED edges (each pair stored once). For user 1, return all users reachable via SHORTEST PATH of length 2 (friends-of-friends only), excluding user 1 themselves and excluding any direct friends. Return `user_id, mutual_count` where mutual_count is the number of distinct user-1 friends that connect to that user_id. Order by mutual_count DESC, user_id ASC.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE friendship (a INTEGER, b INTEGER);
INSERT INTO friendship VALUES (1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(2,5),(2,6),(3,5),(3,7),(4,5),(4,8),(5,9);
questions.model_answer:
WITH edges AS (
  SELECT a AS u, b AS v FROM friendship
  UNION ALL
  SELECT b, a FROM friendship
),
direct AS (
  SELECT v FROM edges WHERE u = 1
)
SELECT e2.v AS user_id, COUNT(DISTINCT e1.v) AS mutual_count
FROM edges e1
JOIN edges e2 ON e1.v = e2.u
WHERE e1.u = 1
  AND e2.v <> 1
  AND e2.v NOT IN (SELECT v FROM direct)
GROUP BY e2.v
ORDER BY mutual_count DESC, user_id;
key_points: Normalize undirected edges to symmetric directed for clean traversal
Two-hop join: e1 from user 1 outward, e2 onward
Exclude self and existing direct friends — these aren't 'recommendations'
COUNT(DISTINCT e1.v) tells you how many of user-1's friends connect to a candidate (Facebook-style 'mutual friends' UX)

questions.prompt: Schema: `experiment_users(experiment, user_id, variant)`. Find users who were enrolled in BOTH experiment 'feed_v2' AND experiment 'pricing_v3', and report the (variant_feed, variant_pricing) crosstab counts. Return `variant_feed, variant_pricing, users`, ordered by variant_feed, variant_pricing.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT f.variant AS variant_feed,
       p.variant AS variant_pricing,
       COUNT(*) AS users
FROM experiment_users f
JOIN experiment_users p
  ON f.user_id = p.user_id
 AND f.experiment = 'feed_v2'
 AND p.experiment = 'pricing_v3'
GROUP BY f.variant, p.variant
ORDER BY variant_feed, variant_pricing;
key_points: Self-join on the same table filtered to the two experiments — implicit set intersection
Group by both variant columns to produce the 2×2 (or 2×N) crosstab
User 5 in feed only — excluded (no pricing row); user 8 nowhere — also absent
Pattern generalizes to N experiments via repeated joins or LATERAL / ARRAY_AGG tricks

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(id, lifetime_value)`. For each customer, compute their PERCENTILE position by LTV (fraction of customers with LTV less than or equal to theirs). Return `id, lifetime_value, percentile`, where percentile is rounded to 3 decimals. Order by lifetime_value DESC, id.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT id, lifetime_value,
       ROUND(CUME_DIST() OVER (ORDER BY lifetime_value)::numeric, 3) AS percentile
FROM customer
ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC, id;
key_points: CUME_DIST: fraction of rows with value <= current row's value (peers share the same value)
Returns (0, 1] and is sensitive to ties — different from PERCENT_RANK which is [0,1)
Customers 3 and 4 tie at 200 → both get the same percentile
ROUND for stable display — comparison engines often diverge on full-precision floats

questions.prompt: Schema: `orders(customer_id, order_date)`. Return customers whose orders span AT LEAST 3 DISTINCT WEEKDAYS (e.g. Monday and Tuesday count as 2 distinct weekdays even if multiple orders happened on each). Use ISO weekday numbering (1=Monday..7=Sunday). Return `customer_id, weekdays_covered`, ordered by weekdays_covered DESC, customer_id ASC.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT customer_id,
       COUNT(DISTINCT EXTRACT(ISODOW FROM order_date)) AS weekdays_covered
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT EXTRACT(ISODOW FROM order_date)) >= 3
ORDER BY weekdays_covered DESC, customer_id;
key_points: EXTRACT(ISODOW FROM ...) — Mon=1..Sun=7 in Postgres
COUNT(DISTINCT EXTRACT(...)) collapses repeated weekday hits
HAVING (not WHERE) — filters aggregate result
Customer 2 has only 2 weekdays, customer 4 only 1 — both excluded

questions.prompt: Schema: `revenue(order_date, amount)`. Aggregate revenue by FISCAL QUARTER, where the fiscal year STARTS ON APRIL 1 (common in UK/India/Japan). FQ1 = Apr–Jun, FQ2 = Jul–Sep, FQ3 = Oct–Dec, FQ4 = Jan–Mar of the next calendar year. Return `fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter, total`, ordered by fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter. fiscal_year is the calendar year in which the fiscal year begins (e.g. FY2025 runs Apr 2025 – Mar 2026).
questions.model_answer:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date) >= 4
       THEN EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date)::int
       ELSE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date)::int - 1
  END AS fiscal_year,
  ((EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date)::int - 4 + 12) % 12) / 3 + 1 AS fiscal_quarter,
  SUM(amount) AS total
FROM revenue
GROUP BY fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter
ORDER BY fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter;
key_points: Fiscal year = calendar year if month >= 4, else previous calendar year
Fiscal quarter via (month − 4 + 12) % 12 / 3 + 1 — wraps Jan–Mar into Q4
All arithmetic on EXTRACT integers; no string formatting needed
Generalizes: for fiscal_start_month K, replace the 4 with K throughout

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(id, name, signup_date)`. As of the reference date '2025-05-14', return customers whose 5-year, 10-year, or 25-year anniversary falls between today and the end of the next 30 days (inclusive, calendar-aware). Return `id, name, milestone_years, anniversary_date`, ordered by anniversary_date, id. If a Feb-29 signup hits a non-leap year, treat the anniversary as Feb 28.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE customer (id INTEGER, name TEXT, signup_date DATE);
