key_points: Put the year filter inside the ON clause -- moving it to WHERE would silently turn the LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN
WHERE o.seller_id IS NULL is the anti-join idiom that surfaces unmatched left rows
Sargable date range (>= and <) rather than YEAR(sale_date) to keep indexes usable
NOT IN would be unsafe if orders.seller_id could be NULL

questions.prompt: Tables `enrolled_employee(emp_id INT, name TEXT)` and `paid_salary(emp_id INT, paid NUMERIC)`. The two tables should normally be in sync. Find employee IDs that appear in exactly ONE of the two tables (employees missing from one side). Return one column `emp_id` ordered ascending. Use a FULL OUTER JOIN.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT COALESCE(e.emp_id, p.emp_id) AS emp_id
FROM enrolled_employee e
FULL OUTER JOIN paid_salary p ON e.emp_id = p.emp_id
WHERE e.emp_id IS NULL OR p.emp_id IS NULL
ORDER BY emp_id;
key_points: FULL OUTER JOIN keeps rows from both sides even when there's no match
WHERE (left IS NULL OR right IS NULL) isolates the asymmetric rows
COALESCE merges the two emp_id columns into one output column
Common interview pattern -- usually solved instead with two UNION'd anti-joins

questions.prompt: Tables `actor_director(actor_id INT, director_id INT, movie_id INT)`. Return pairs of (actor_id, director_id) who have worked together on AT LEAST 3 distinct movies. Columns: `actor_id, director_id`, ordered by both ascending.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE actor_director (actor_id INTEGER, director_id INTEGER, movie_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO actor_director VALUES
  (1,1,10),(1,1,11),(1,1,12),
  (1,2,20),(1,2,21),
  (2,1,30),(2,1,31),(2,1,32),(2,1,33),
  (3,3,40),(3,3,41),(3,3,42),
  (3,3,42);
questions.model_answer:
SELECT actor_id, director_id
FROM actor_director
GROUP BY actor_id, director_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT movie_id) >= 3
ORDER BY actor_id, director_id;
key_points: GROUP BY two columns simultaneously
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT movie_id) handles the duplicate (3,3,42) row correctly
COUNT(*) would incorrectly inflate that pair's count by counting the duplicate row
Filter is on aggregate -> HAVING (not WHERE)

questions.prompt: Table `cinema(seat_id INT, free CHAR(1))` lists every seat in a row with a 'Y'/'N' availability flag. Seat IDs are consecutive integers but not necessarily starting at 1. Return all `seat_id`s that are FREE AND have at least one ADJACENT free seat (immediately before or after). Order by `seat_id`. No duplicate output.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT DISTINCT a.seat_id
FROM cinema a
JOIN cinema b
  ON ABS(a.seat_id - b.seat_id) = 1
 AND a.free = 'Y' AND b.free = 'Y'
ORDER BY a.seat_id;
key_points: Self-join with ABS(diff) = 1 captures both neighbors (left or right)
Filter both rows to free = 'Y'
DISTINCT to avoid double-counting seats with two free neighbors (e.g. seat 7)
Alternative with LAG/LEAD must OR the two predicates

questions.prompt: Tables `friendship(user1_id INT, user2_id INT)` (mutual; stored only one direction) and `likes(user_id INT, page_id INT)`. Recommend pages to user 1: every page that any of user 1's FRIENDS has liked, EXCLUDING pages user 1 already likes. Output one column `recommended_page` of DISTINCT page ids, ordered ascending.
questions.model_answer:
WITH friends AS (
  SELECT user2_id AS friend FROM friendship WHERE user1_id = 1
  UNION
  SELECT user1_id        FROM friendship WHERE user2_id = 1
)
SELECT DISTINCT l.page_id AS recommended_page
FROM friends f
JOIN likes l ON l.user_id = f.friend
WHERE l.page_id NOT IN (SELECT page_id FROM likes WHERE user_id = 1)
ORDER BY recommended_page;
key_points: Symmetrize the friendship by UNIONing both directions
UNION (not UNION ALL) implicitly dedupes -- DISTINCT not needed inside friends
JOIN to likes for the friends' liked pages
NOT IN safe here because user 1's likes never contain NULL page_id
Final DISTINCT collapses pages liked by multiple friends

questions.prompt: Table `activity(player_id INT, device_id INT, event_date DATE)` records every game session. For each player, return the device_id they used on their EARLIEST event_date. If a player used multiple devices on that earliest date, return the SMALLEST device_id. Columns: `player_id, device_id`, ordered by `player_id`.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE activity (player_id INTEGER, device_id INTEGER, event_date DATE);
INSERT INTO activity VALUES
  (1,10,'2025-01-01'),
  (1,20,'2025-01-02'),
  (2,30,'2025-02-01'),
  (2,40,'2025-02-01'),
  (3,50,'2025-03-05');
questions.model_answer:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT player_id, device_id, event_date,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date, device_id) AS rn
  FROM activity
)
SELECT player_id, device_id
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY player_id;
key_points: ROW_NUMBER over (PARTITION BY player ORDER BY date, device) ensures deterministic tie-break
WHERE rn = 1 picks the earliest event
Tuple-match alternative: (player_id, event_date) IN (SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date)...) -- needs an extra step to break ties

questions.prompt: Table `match(match_id INT, host_team INT, guest_team INT, host_goals INT, guest_goals INT)`. Scoring: a win = 3 pts, a draw = 1 pt, a loss = 0 pts (a team gets points for each match it played, whether as host or guest). Compute total points per team. Output columns: `team_id, points`, ordered by `points` DESC then `team_id` ASC. Include teams that never won (zero/draw-only) but EXCLUDE team ids that never played.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE match (match_id INTEGER, host_team INTEGER, guest_team INTEGER, host_goals INTEGER, guest_goals INTEGER);
INSERT INTO match VALUES
  (1,10,20,3,1),
  (2,10,30,2,2),
  (3,20,30,0,4),
  (4,40,10,1,1),
  (5,30,20,1,0);
questions.model_answer:
WITH legs AS (
  SELECT host_team AS team,
         CASE WHEN host_goals > guest_goals THEN 3
              WHEN host_goals = guest_goals THEN 1
              ELSE 0 END AS pts
  FROM match
  UNION ALL
  SELECT guest_team,
         CASE WHEN guest_goals > host_goals THEN 3
              WHEN host_goals = guest_goals THEN 1
              ELSE 0 END
  FROM match
)
SELECT team AS team_id, SUM(pts)::int AS points
FROM legs
GROUP BY team
ORDER BY points DESC, team_id;
key_points: UNION ALL stacks each team's host- and guest-side experience as separate rows
CASE distinguishes win / draw / loss for each side
GROUP BY team gives total points
Teams that never played simply don't appear (no rows)
Alternative: a single SELECT with a CASE that aggregates over both sides simultaneously

questions.prompt: Table `products(product_id INT, store1 NUMERIC, store2 NUMERIC, store3 NUMERIC)` stores prices for the SAME product in three stores. Some store prices can be NULL (the product isn't sold there). UNPIVOT into long format: columns `product_id, store, price`. Skip rows where price IS NULL. Order by `product_id, store`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT product_id, 'store1' AS store, store1 AS price FROM products WHERE store1 IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT product_id, 'store2',        store2        FROM products WHERE store2 IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT product_id, 'store3',        store3        FROM products WHERE store3 IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY product_id, store;
key_points: Pivot-LONG via UNION ALL of one SELECT per source column
Filter NULL within each branch to drop missing prices
Single ORDER BY at the bottom of the UNION ALL applies to the whole result
Product 4 (all NULL) is correctly excluded entirely

questions.prompt: Table `exam(exam_id INT, student_id INT, score INT)` and `student(student_id INT, student_name TEXT)`. A 'quiet' student is one who is NEVER the maximum scorer and NEVER the minimum scorer in any exam they took -- AND has taken at least one exam. Return `student_id, student_name`, ordered by `student_id`. If two students tie for top in an exam, they BOTH disqualify.
questions.model_answer:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT exam_id, student_id,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY exam_id ORDER BY score DESC) AS rk_high,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY exam_id ORDER BY score ASC)  AS rk_low
  FROM exam
),
loud AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT student_id FROM ranked WHERE rk_high = 1 OR rk_low = 1
)
SELECT s.student_id, s.student_name
FROM student s
JOIN exam e ON e.student_id = s.student_id
WHERE s.student_id NOT IN (SELECT student_id FROM loud)
GROUP BY s.student_id, s.student_name
ORDER BY s.student_id;
key_points: DENSE_RANK in BOTH directions per exam -- ties at the top/bottom both register as rank 1
Collect the union of 'top' and 'bottom' students into a 'loud' set
Inner-join to exam first so students with zero exams are excluded
Final filter: NOT IN loud AND HAS exam participation

questions.prompt: Tables `country(country_id INT, country_name TEXT, country_code TEXT)`, `person(person_id INT, phone_number TEXT)` -- phone numbers begin with the country_code followed by '-'. `calls(caller_id INT, callee_id INT, duration INT)`. Return country names where the AVERAGE call duration of calls touching that country (either caller or callee in that country) STRICTLY exceeds the GLOBAL average across all call legs. Output one column `country_name` ordered ascending.
questions.model_answer:
WITH legs AS (
  SELECT caller_id AS pid, duration FROM calls
  UNION ALL
  SELECT callee_id, duration FROM calls
),
tagged AS (
  SELECT c.country_name, l.duration
  FROM legs l
  JOIN person p ON p.person_id = l.pid
  JOIN country c ON split_part(p.phone_number, '-', 1) = c.country_code
)
SELECT country_name
FROM tagged
GROUP BY country_name
HAVING AVG(duration) > (SELECT AVG(duration) FROM legs)
ORDER BY country_name;
key_points: Build a 'legs' set that doubles each call (once for caller, once for callee)
Extract country prefix using split_part and join through person to country
Subquery in HAVING computes the GLOBAL average across all legs -- same denominator as group averages
Use STRICT inequality (>) so a country exactly at the global mean is not flagged

questions.prompt: Table `employee(emp_id INT, name TEXT, dept_id INT, salary INT)`. For each department, return the row(s) where the salary equals the department's MAX salary. Use a tuple-match subquery (no window functions). Output columns: `dept_id, name, salary`. Multiple winners per department all appear. Order by `dept_id`, then `name`.
key_points: Tuple matching (dept_id, salary) IN (...) is a clean idiom that naturally returns ALL tied winners
MAX(salary) is computed per department in the subquery
Window-function alternative: DENSE_RANK = 1 per partition -- same result, different mechanism
ROW_NUMBER = 1 would lose ties; DENSE_RANK = 1 retains them

questions.prompt: Table `customers(customer_id INT, name TEXT)` and `orders(order_id INT, customer_id INT, order_date DATE)`. Return the customer (or customers, if tied) with the LARGEST number of orders. Columns: `customer_id, name, order_count`. Order by `customer_id`. Use a window function so ties surface naturally.
questions.model_answer:
WITH counts AS (
  SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT customer_id, order_count,
         RANK() OVER (ORDER BY order_count DESC) AS rk
  FROM counts
)
SELECT r.customer_id, c.name, r.order_count::int AS order_count
FROM ranked r
JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id
WHERE rk = 1
ORDER BY r.customer_id;
key_points: Compute per-customer counts first, then rank them
RANK = 1 returns every customer tied for the lead (LIMIT 1 would drop ties)
DENSE_RANK works equivalently here since rk=1 only ever has one tier
LIMIT 1 ORDER BY count DESC is the naive solution -- explicitly broken on ties

questions.prompt: Table `student_courses(student_id INT, course_id INT, grade INT)`. For each student, return the course where they got the HIGHEST grade. Ties: prefer the SMALLEST course_id. Columns: `student_id, course_id, grade`, ordered by `student_id`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT student_id, course_id, grade,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY grade DESC, course_id ASC) AS rn
  FROM student_courses
)
SELECT student_id, course_id, grade
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY student_id;
key_points: ROW_NUMBER not DENSE_RANK -- we want EXACTLY ONE row per student (tie-broken)
ORDER BY grade DESC, course_id ASC inside the window encodes the tie-break rule
Without the course_id tie-break, the engine is free to return any of the tied rows non-deterministically
Filter rn=1 in the outer query

questions.prompt: Table `users(user_id INT, email TEXT)`. Return DUPLICATE email addresses (emails that appear two or more times) -- compare case-INSENSITIVELY (so 'A@x.com' and 'a@x.com' are the same). Columns: `email_lower, occurrences`. Order by `email_lower`.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE users (user_id INTEGER, email TEXT);
INSERT INTO users VALUES
  (1,'a@x.com'),
  (2,'A@X.com'),
  (3,'b@x.com'),
  (4,'c@x.com'),
  (5,'C@X.COM'),
  (6,'C@x.com');
questions.model_answer:
SELECT LOWER(email) AS email_lower, COUNT(*)::int AS occurrences
FROM users
GROUP BY LOWER(email)
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY email_lower;
key_points: GROUP BY LOWER(email) collapses case-equivalent rows
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 keeps only duplicates
Same expression must appear in SELECT and GROUP BY (no column-alias reuse in GROUP BY in standard SQL)
Wrapping in a function defeats indexes -- in production, use a functional index on LOWER(email)

questions.prompt: Table `events(event_id INT, ts TIMESTAMP)` logs user events. Bucket each event into a 15-minute window starting at the top of the hour. For each bucket, return the event count. Format the bucket as the START timestamp of the 15-minute window. Columns: `bucket_start, event_count`. Order by `bucket_start`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT date_trunc('hour', ts)
       + (EXTRACT(MINUTE FROM ts)::int / 15) * INTERVAL '15 minutes' AS bucket_start,
       COUNT(*)::int AS event_count
FROM events
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
key_points: date_trunc('hour', ts) gives the floor of the hour
(MINUTE / 15) * 15 minutes adds the appropriate quarter offset
Alternative: date_bin('15 minutes', ts, '...epoch...') in Postgres 14+
GROUP BY 1 references the computed bucket -- works in Postgres

questions.prompt: Table `daily_leads(date_id DATE, make_name TEXT, lead_id INT, partner_id INT)`. For each (date, make), return the number of DISTINCT leads and the number of DISTINCT partners. Columns: `date_id, make_name, unique_leads, unique_partners`, ordered by `date_id, make_name`.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE daily_leads (date_id DATE, make_name TEXT, lead_id INTEGER, partner_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO daily_leads VALUES
  ('2025-01-01','Toyota',1,10),
  ('2025-01-01','Toyota',1,10),
  ('2025-01-01','Toyota',2,11),
  ('2025-01-01','Honda',5,20),
  ('2025-01-02','Toyota',1,12);
questions.model_answer:
SELECT date_id, make_name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id)::int    AS unique_leads,
       COUNT(DISTINCT partner_id)::int AS unique_partners
FROM daily_leads
GROUP BY date_id, make_name
ORDER BY date_id, make_name;
key_points: COUNT(DISTINCT col) removes duplicate values inside the group
GROUP BY (date_id, make_name) -- both grouping columns
COUNT(DISTINCT) cannot use multiple columns directly -- you'd need (col1, col2) or concat for that

questions.prompt: Table `login(user_id INT, login_date DATE)` records distinct daily logins. Find users who have logged in on 3 OR MORE CONSECUTIVE calendar days. Use a LAG-BASED 3-row pattern: check whether (this_date - 1) and (this_date - 2) both appear as preceding logins for the same user. Return distinct `user_id`s, ordered ascending.
questions.model_answer:
WITH dt AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id, login_date FROM login
),
shifted AS (
  SELECT user_id, login_date,
         LAG(login_date, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS d1,
         LAG(login_date, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS d2
  FROM dt
)
SELECT DISTINCT user_id
FROM shifted
WHERE login_date - d1 = 1 AND login_date - d2 = 2
ORDER BY user_id;
key_points: Two LAGs by offsets 1 and 2 over the per-user-ordered date stream
Equality checks: today - 1 = yesterday AND today - 2 = day-before
Postgres date subtraction yields integer days directly
DISTINCT user_id at the outermost level dedupes when a user has multiple qualifying triples
Different style from gaps-and-islands -- this is a 'sliding 3-row check'

questions.prompt: Table `orders(order_id INT, customer_id INT, product_id INT, order_date DATE)`. For each customer, return the product they ORDERED MOST OFTEN. If a customer has multiple products tied for most-ordered, return ALL tied products. Columns: `customer_id, product_id, times`, ordered by `customer_id, product_id`. The same customer-product combo can appear on different dates.
questions.model_answer:
WITH counts AS (
  SELECT customer_id, product_id, COUNT(*) AS times
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id, product_id
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT customer_id, product_id, times,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY times DESC) AS rk
  FROM counts
)
SELECT customer_id, product_id, times::int AS times
FROM ranked
WHERE rk = 1
ORDER BY customer_id, product_id;
key_points: Two-step: count per (customer, product), then RANK per customer
RANK() so ties at rank 1 are all returned (DENSE_RANK works the same here)
ROW_NUMBER would drop tied products non-deterministically
PARTITION BY customer_id so each customer is ranked independently

questions.prompt: Table `logins(user_id INT, time_stamp TIMESTAMP)`. Return the LATEST login timestamp for each user that occurred during the year 2025. Columns: `user_id, last_stamp`. Users with no 2025 logins must NOT appear. Order by `user_id`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT user_id, MAX(time_stamp) AS last_stamp
FROM logins
WHERE time_stamp >= TIMESTAMP '2025-01-01' AND time_stamp < TIMESTAMP '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id;
key_points: Sargable range filter (>=, <) on the timestamp column -- keeps indexes usable
AVOID EXTRACT(YEAR FROM ts) = 2025 which can't use an index
MAX(time_stamp) per user inside the filtered set
Users with no 2025 logins (user 3) are dropped because they have zero rows after WHERE

questions.prompt: Table `employees(employee_id INT, name TEXT, salary NUMERIC)`. Pay a 'special bonus' equal to the employee's salary IF AND ONLY IF: (a) employee_id is ODD AND (b) name does not start with letter 'M' (case-sensitive). Everyone else gets bonus = 0. Return columns: `employee_id, bonus`, ordered by `employee_id`.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, name TEXT, salary NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice',100),
  (2,'Bob',200),
  (3,'Maria',300),
  (4,'Mike',400),
  (5,'Cara',500),
  (6,'Diana',600),
  (7,'Mira',700),
  (9,'mike',900);
questions.model_answer:
SELECT employee_id,
       CASE WHEN employee_id % 2 = 1 AND name NOT LIKE 'M%' THEN salary ELSE 0 END AS bonus
FROM employees
ORDER BY employee_id;
key_points: % (modulo) for odd check; NOT LIKE 'M%' for case-sensitive name prefix
LIKE in Postgres is case-sensitive (ILIKE is case-insensitive)
'mike' (lowercase m) DOES get a bonus because the prefix check is case-sensitive -- intentional gotcha
Single CASE WHEN handles all cases inline

questions.prompt: You need the top 3 salaries per department but want EVERY row that ties at the third level (so a 4-way tie at salary #3 returns 6 rows total: rank 1, rank 2, four ranked at the tied 3rd). Which window function should you use as the predicate?
options: ROW_NUMBER() <= 3
RANK() <= 3
DENSE_RANK() <= 3
NTILE(3) = 1
questions.explanation: DENSE_RANK assigns sequential ranks (1,2,3,3,3,4) without skipping, so 'rank <= 3' keeps every salary tier up to the third distinct salary -- including all ties at any tier. ROW_NUMBER would cap the total at exactly 3 rows. RANK skips after a tie (1,1,3 then 4...) and could MISS the third tier entirely if there's a tie at rank 2 (e.g. 1,2,2,4 -- rank 3 is absent). NTILE(3) buckets rows into thirds by count, not by value level.

questions.prompt:
You want sellers who had NO orders in 2025. You write:

```sql
SELECT s.seller_name FROM seller s
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.seller_id = s.seller_id
WHERE o.sale_date NOT BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-12-31'
  AND o.seller_id IS NULL;
```

Why is this query broken?
options: BETWEEN is inclusive on both endpoints, so it misses December 31

The WHERE on `o.sale_date` is evaluated AFTER the LEFT JOIN's NULL rows are introduced, but `NOT BETWEEN` against NULL returns NULL (not TRUE), filtering those rows out -- so sellers with no orders at all disappear
LEFT JOIN doesn't preserve unmatched left rows in Postgres
Sellers with orders in other years would be incorrectly INCLUDED in the result
questions.explanation: Three-valued logic strikes: when a seller has zero orders, the LEFT JOIN produces a row where every `o.*` column is NULL. `NULL NOT BETWEEN x AND y` is NULL, not TRUE, so the WHERE clause drops the row -- defeating the entire point of the anti-join. The fix is to move the year predicate into the ON clause: `ON o.seller_id = s.seller_id AND o.sale_date >= '2025-01-01' AND o.sale_date < '2026-01-01'`, then keep only `WHERE o.seller_id IS NULL`.

questions.prompt: You need to find ALL pairs of overlapping sessions for the same account (different IPs). Which approaches CORRECTLY identify overlap, regardless of how many sessions exist between the two overlappers? (Select all that apply)
options: Self-join on (account_id, l1.login BETWEEN l2.login AND l2.logout, l1.ip <> l2.ip)
LAG over (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY login_ts) and check overlap between consecutive rows only
Self-join + tsrange `&&` overlap operator (Postgres): `l1.account_id = l2.account_id AND l1.ip <> l2.ip AND tsrange(l1.login_ts, l1.logout_ts) && tsrange(l2.login_ts, l2.logout_ts)`
MAX(logout_ts) running window vs current login_ts -- check if current login < max-of-previous-logouts
questions.explanation: Self-join across all pairs (option A) handles arbitrarily-distant overlaps. Range types with `&&` (option C) are the cleanest Postgres-native solution. The running-MAX(logout) trick (option D) extends the LAG idea to handle the 'tail overlap' case across non-consecutive rows. The plain LAG-only approach (B) is the canonical TRAP: it only compares the immediate predecessor, so if session 1 (IP A) overlaps with session 3 (IP B) but session 2 (IP A) lies between them chronologically, the overlap escapes detection.

questions.prompt: You want to find customers who bought BOTH product A AND product B but NEVER product C. Which clause is the cleanest, most correct Postgres expression?
options: WHERE product IN ('A','B') AND product NOT IN ('C')
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN product='A' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 AND ...
HAVING COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE product='A') > 0 AND COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE product='B') > 0 AND COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE product='C') = 0
WHERE product='A' AND product='B' AND product<>'C'
questions.explanation: `FILTER (WHERE ...)` per aggregate is the Postgres-idiomatic, clean way to write conditional aggregation. The HAVING-with-SUM(CASE) form is equivalent but more verbose. Option A is wrong because IN/NOT IN apply per row, not per customer-group. Option D is impossible (the same row can't have product='A' AND product='B').

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(cust_code, recorded_outstanding)` (the snapshot value stored on the customer row), `orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount)`, `payment(pay_id, cust_code, paid_amount)`. Reconcile by computing `calculated_balance = SUM(ord_amount) - SUM(paid_amount)` and `discrepancy = recorded_outstanding - calculated_balance`. Return only customers whose `discrepancy` is non-zero (i.e. their stored value disagrees with the derived value). Return `cust_code, recorded_outstanding, total_orders, total_payments, calculated_balance, discrepancy`. NULLs from missing orders/payments treated as 0. Order by `ABS(discrepancy)` DESC, then `cust_code`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH o AS (
  SELECT cust_code, SUM(ord_amount) AS total_orders FROM orders GROUP BY cust_code
),
p AS (
  SELECT cust_code, SUM(paid_amount) AS total_payments FROM payment GROUP BY cust_code
)
SELECT c.cust_code,
       c.recorded_outstanding,
       COALESCE(o.total_orders,0)   AS total_orders,
       COALESCE(p.total_payments,0) AS total_payments,
       COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0) AS calculated_balance,
       c.recorded_outstanding - (COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0)) AS discrepancy
FROM customer c
LEFT JOIN o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
LEFT JOIN p ON p.cust_code = c.cust_code
WHERE c.recorded_outstanding - (COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0)) <> 0
ORDER BY ABS(c.recorded_outstanding - (COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0))) DESC, c.cust_code;
key_points: Pre-aggregate orders and payments in separate CTEs (otherwise the join multiplies rows)
LEFT JOIN so customers with zero orders or payments still surface
Filter to non-zero discrepancy in WHERE — same expression repeated (or wrap in a subquery)
ABS() for ordering by magnitude of discrepancy

questions.prompt: Schema: `product(product_id, name)` is the FULL product catalog. `purchase(customer_id, product_id)` records who bought what (duplicates allowed). Find customers who have bought EVERY product in the catalog at least once (classic relational division). Return `customer_id`, ordered ascending. Edge case: if the catalog is empty, every customer trivially qualifies.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT customer_id
FROM purchase
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM product)
ORDER BY customer_id;
key_points: Classic relational-division pattern: COUNT(DISTINCT bought) = COUNT(catalog)
DISTINCT is essential — repeated purchases of the same product must not inflate the count

Edge case: if catalog count = 0, ANY customer with at least one purchase would NOT qualify (because they'd have COUNT > 0); to truly handle 'every customer qualifies', you'd need a different formulation (e.g. NOT EXISTS-of-the-difference)
Alternative formulation: NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM product p WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM purchase WHERE customer_id = c.customer_id AND product_id = p.product_id))

questions.prompt: Schema: `course(course_id, name)` lists all required courses. `enrollment(student_id, course_id, grade)` records enrollments where `grade` is a letter ('A','B','C','D','F') or NULL (in progress). Find students who have COMPLETED (i.e. grade IS NOT NULL AND grade <> 'F') every required course. A student who has merely enrolled in but not passed a course does not qualify. Return `student_id`, ordered ascending.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT student_id
FROM enrollment
WHERE grade IS NOT NULL AND grade <> 'F'
GROUP BY student_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT course_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM course)
ORDER BY student_id;
key_points: Filter to PASSED enrollments BEFORE aggregating (WHERE grade IS NOT NULL AND <> 'F')
Student 2 fails because they got 'F' in Physics; student 3 fails because Physics is NULL (in progress); student 5 fails because they only completed 1 course
COUNT(DISTINCT course_id) protects against any accidental duplicate enrollment rows
Compare to the total course count from the master `course` table

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(cust_code, cust_name, cust_country)` and `orders(ord_num, cust_code)`. Return customers who have NEVER placed an order. Use the LEFT JOIN / IS NULL anti-join pattern (NOT IN is unsafe with NULLs and NOT EXISTS is fine but not what we want to test here). Return `cust_code, cust_name, cust_country`, ordered by `cust_code`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT c.cust_code, c.cust_name, c.cust_country
FROM customer c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
WHERE o.cust_code IS NULL
ORDER BY c.cust_code;
key_points: LEFT JOIN orders, then filter WHERE orders.<key> IS NULL
Equivalent to NOT EXISTS but uses join semantics
Avoid NOT IN — if any row in orders.cust_code were NULL, the outer query would return zero rows
Test the IS NULL on a non-nullable column from the right side (the FK, not a nullable attribute)

questions.prompt: Schema: `agent(agent_code, working_area, commission)`. For each working area, compute `num_agents`, `avg_commission` (rounded to 4 decimals), `min_commission`, `max_commission`, and `commission_spread` = max-min. Then RANK areas by avg_commission DESC (ties get the same rank, no gaps — use DENSE_RANK). Return only areas where `num_agents >= 1` (i.e. all of them) with columns `working_area, num_agents, avg_commission, min_commission, max_commission, commission_spread, rk`, ordered by rk then working_area.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE agent (agent_code TEXT, working_area TEXT, commission NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO agent VALUES
  ('A001','Bangalore',0.14),
  ('A002','Mumbai',0.11),
  ('A003','Bangalore',0.13),
  ('A004','Toronto',0.15),
  ('A005','London',0.13),
  ('A006','London',0.15),
  ('A007','Bangalore',0.15),
  ('A008','New York',0.12),
  ('A009','Hampshair',0.11),
  ('A010','Chennai',0.14),
  ('A011','Brisban',0.13),
  ('A012','San Jose',0.12);
questions.model_answer:
WITH g AS (
  SELECT working_area,
         COUNT(*) AS num_agents,
         ROUND(AVG(commission)::numeric, 4) AS avg_commission,
         MIN(commission) AS min_commission,
         MAX(commission) AS max_commission,
         MAX(commission) - MIN(commission) AS commission_spread
  FROM agent
  GROUP BY working_area
)
SELECT working_area, num_agents, avg_commission, min_commission, max_commission, commission_spread,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY avg_commission DESC) AS rk
FROM g
ORDER BY rk, working_area;
key_points: Aggregate first in a CTE, then apply a window function over the aggregated rows
DENSE_RANK groups ties together with no gaps (1, 2, 2, 2, 3 — not 1, 2, 2, 2, 5)
Postgres: AVG(numeric) returns numeric, but cast explicitly before ROUND for safety
Spread = MAX − MIN summarizes within-area variance without needing variance/stddev

questions.topic: Top-N Per Group with Multi-key Tie-break
questions.prompt: Schema: `orders(ord_num, agent_code, ord_amount, ord_date)`. For each agent, return the TOP 2 orders by amount DESC. Tie-break: earlier `ord_date` wins; further ties broken by lower `ord_num`. If an agent has only one order, return that one row. Return `agent_code, ord_num, ord_date, ord_amount, rk`, ordered by `agent_code, rk`. Use ROW_NUMBER (no ties surface a duplicate rank).
questions.model_answer:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT agent_code, ord_num, ord_date, ord_amount,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY agent_code ORDER BY ord_amount DESC, ord_date ASC, ord_num ASC) AS rk
  FROM orders
)
SELECT agent_code, ord_num, ord_date, ord_amount, rk
FROM ranked
WHERE rk <= 2
ORDER BY agent_code, rk;
key_points: ROW_NUMBER (not RANK/DENSE_RANK) — we want EXACTLY two rows per agent
Multi-key ORDER BY in the OVER() clause: amount DESC, then date ASC, then ord_num ASC
Filter rk <= 2 in OUTER query — you cannot reference window aliases in WHERE
Agents with only one order surface a single row (rk = 1)

questions.prompt: Schema: `category(category_id, name, parent_id)` forms a tree (parent_id is NULL at the root). `product(product_id, category_id, sales)` links products to LEAF categories only. For every category in the tree (leaf AND internal), compute the SUM of sales of all products in its subtree (itself + every descendant). Return `category_id, name, total_sales`, ordered by `category_id`. Categories with no products in their subtree get 0.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE category (category_id INT, name TEXT, parent_id INT);
INSERT INTO category VALUES
  (1,'All',NULL),
  (2,'Electronics',1),
  (3,'Phones',2),
  (4,'Laptops',2),
  (5,'Apparel',1),
  (6,'Shoes',5),
  (7,'Shirts',5);
CREATE TABLE product (product_id INT, category_id INT, sales NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO product VALUES
  (101,3,500),(102,3,300),
  (103,4,1000),
  (104,6,200),
  (105,7,400),(106,7,100);
questions.model_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE descendants(ancestor_id, descendant_id) AS (
  SELECT category_id, category_id FROM category
  UNION ALL
  SELECT d.ancestor_id, c.category_id
  FROM descendants d JOIN category c ON c.parent_id = d.descendant_id
)
SELECT c.category_id, c.name,
       COALESCE(SUM(p.sales), 0) AS total_sales
FROM category c
LEFT JOIN descendants d ON d.ancestor_id = c.category_id
LEFT JOIN product p ON p.category_id = d.descendant_id
GROUP BY c.category_id, c.name
ORDER BY c.category_id;
key_points: Recursive CTE materializes the transitive (ancestor, descendant) closure including the trivial self-pair
Anchor: each category is its own descendant (so SUM picks up products in the category itself)
LEFT JOIN to product ensures internal categories with NO direct products still get descendants' sales
Group by the ancestor; SUM rolls up the entire subtree

questions.prompt: Schema: `employee(emp_id, name, manager_id)` where the CEO has manager_id = NULL. For every employee, return the FULL path from the CEO down to them as a `>`-separated string of names, plus their `depth` (CEO is 0). Return `emp_id, name, depth, path`, ordered by `depth` then `emp_id`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE tree AS (
  SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 0 AS depth, name AS path
  FROM employee WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, t.depth + 1, t.path || ' > ' || e.name
  FROM employee e JOIN tree t ON e.manager_id = t.emp_id
)
SELECT emp_id, name, depth, path FROM tree ORDER BY depth, emp_id;
key_points: Recursive CTE: anchor is the CEO (manager_id IS NULL) at depth 0
Recursive step: append current employee's name to parent's path with ' > '
Increment depth by 1 on each step
ORDER BY depth lists employees breadth-first; secondary sort by emp_id is deterministic

questions.prompt: Schema: `edge(src, dst)` is a DIRECTED graph that MAY contain cycles. From node 1, return every node reachable via any directed path (excluding node 1 itself), with the MINIMUM hop count to reach it. The traversal must terminate even if the graph has cycles — track the visited path. Return `node, min_hops`, ordered by `node`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE walk(node, hops, path) AS (
  SELECT 1, 0, ARRAY[1]
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.dst, w.hops + 1, w.path || e.dst
  FROM walk w JOIN edge e ON e.src = w.node
  WHERE NOT (e.dst = ANY(w.path))
)
SELECT node, MIN(hops) AS min_hops
FROM walk
WHERE node <> 1
GROUP BY node
ORDER BY node;
key_points: Recursive CTE: maintain an array of visited nodes as `path`
Guard the recursive step with NOT (e.dst = ANY(path)) — prevents infinite cycles
Aggregate at the end with MIN(hops) to collapse multiple paths to the same node
Postgres ARRAY operators: ARRAY[1] initializer, || to append, = ANY() to test membership

questions.prompt: Schema: `bom(parent, child, qty)` describes how each parent assembly is composed of child parts (with quantities). The graph is a DAG (no cycles), rooted at 'Bike'. For ONE bike, recursively compute the total quantity of EACH leaf material required. A leaf is any `child` that never appears as a `parent`. Return `leaf_material, total_qty`, ordered by `leaf_material`.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE bom (parent TEXT, child TEXT, qty NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO bom VALUES
  ('Bike','Frame',1),
  ('Bike','Wheel',2),
  ('Bike','Handlebar',1),
  ('Frame','SteelTube',5),
  ('Frame','Bolt',12),
  ('Wheel','Tire',1),
  ('Wheel','Spoke',32),
  ('Wheel','Bolt',6),
  ('Handlebar','Grip',2),
  ('Handlebar','SteelTube',1);
questions.model_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE explode(part, qty) AS (
  SELECT child, qty FROM bom WHERE parent = 'Bike'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT b.child, e.qty * b.qty
  FROM explode e JOIN bom b ON b.parent = e.part
),
leaves AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT child FROM bom
  EXCEPT
  SELECT DISTINCT parent FROM bom
)
SELECT e.part AS leaf_material, SUM(e.qty) AS total_qty
FROM explode e JOIN leaves l ON l.child = e.part
GROUP BY e.part
ORDER BY e.part;
key_points: Recursive CTE multiplies quantities at each level (e.qty * b.qty)
Identify leaves as `child set EXCEPT parent set`
Sum across all paths reaching each leaf — a leaf can be reached via multiple parents (Bolt is in both Frame and Wheel; SteelTube is in both Frame and Handlebar)
Spokes: 2 wheels * 32 spokes each = 64; Bolts: 1*12 (Frame) + 2*6 (Wheels) = 24

questions.prompt: Schema: `revenue(d, amount)` — daily revenue. Aggregate revenue by FISCAL QUARTER, where the fiscal year STARTS ON APRIL 1 (common UK/India/Japan calendar). FQ1 = Apr–Jun, FQ2 = Jul–Sep, FQ3 = Oct–Dec, FQ4 = Jan–Mar of the FOLLOWING calendar year. `fiscal_year` is the calendar year in which the fiscal year STARTS (so revenue in March 2026 belongs to fiscal_year 2025, FQ4). Return `fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter, total_amount`, ordered by `fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) >= 4 THEN EXTRACT(YEAR FROM d)::INT
       ELSE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM d)::INT - 1
  END AS fiscal_year,
  CASE
    WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) BETWEEN 4 AND 6  THEN 1
    WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) BETWEEN 7 AND 9  THEN 2
    WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) BETWEEN 10 AND 12 THEN 3
    ELSE 4
  END AS fiscal_quarter,
  SUM(amount) AS total_amount
FROM revenue
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter;
key_points: fiscal_year = calendar_year if month >= April; else calendar_year - 1 (so January 2026 belongs to FY 2025)
fiscal_quarter mapping: Apr-Jun=1, Jul-Sep=2, Oct-Dec=3, Jan-Mar=4
EXTRACT(MONTH/YEAR FROM d) is portable Postgres
Watch the integer cast: EXTRACT returns numeric in Postgres

questions.prompt: Schema: `survey(question_id, respondent_id, answer)`. Pivot to one row per respondent, with one column per question containing that respondent's answer. The set of questions is small and known: q1, q2, q3. Use conditional aggregation. If a respondent didn't answer a question, the cell should be NULL. Return `respondent_id, q1, q2, q3` ordered by respondent_id.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT respondent_id,
       MAX(CASE WHEN question_id = 'q1' THEN answer END) AS q1,
       MAX(CASE WHEN question_id = 'q2' THEN answer END) AS q2,
       MAX(CASE WHEN question_id = 'q3' THEN answer END) AS q3
FROM survey
GROUP BY respondent_id
ORDER BY respondent_id;
key_points: Conditional aggregation pattern: MAX(CASE WHEN ...) per pivoted column
MAX works because CASE returns NULL for non-matching rows; MAX(NULL, value) = value
Missing answers naturally become NULL (the CASE returns NULL for every row when no answer exists)
Portable across all engines — does not rely on PIVOT operator

questions.prompt: Schema: `agent(agent_code, commission)`, `customer(cust_code, agent_code)`, `orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount)`. For each agent, compute the TOTAL order amount placed by THEIR customers, the agent's commission earned (total * commission rate), and the AVERAGE order size across those customers (rounded to 2 dp). Return `agent_code, num_orders, total_amount, commission_earned, avg_order`, ordered by `total_amount` DESC then `agent_code`. Exclude agents with zero orders.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT a.agent_code,
       COUNT(o.ord_num) AS num_orders,
       SUM(o.ord_amount) AS total_amount,
       ROUND((SUM(o.ord_amount) * a.commission)::numeric, 2) AS commission_earned,
       ROUND(AVG(o.ord_amount)::numeric, 2) AS avg_order
FROM agent a
JOIN customer c ON c.agent_code = a.agent_code
JOIN orders o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
GROUP BY a.agent_code, a.commission
ORDER BY total_amount DESC, a.agent_code;
key_points: Three-table join: agent → customer → orders
Group by agent_code AND commission (commission is functionally dependent but Postgres requires it in GROUP BY unless using a derived table)
Inner joins drop agents with no orders (intentional per problem)
Cast to numeric before ROUND with a scale argument

questions.prompt: Schema: `salary(emp_id, dept, amount)`. Find employees who earn MORE than the AVERAGE salary OF THEIR OWN DEPARTMENT. Return `emp_id, dept, amount, dept_avg`, ordered by `dept` then `emp_id`. Round `dept_avg` to 2 decimals.
questions.model_answer:
WITH d AS (
  SELECT dept, AVG(amount) AS dept_avg FROM salary GROUP BY dept
)
SELECT s.emp_id, s.dept, s.amount, ROUND(d.dept_avg::numeric, 2) AS dept_avg
FROM salary s JOIN d ON d.dept = s.dept
WHERE s.amount > d.dept_avg
ORDER BY s.dept, s.emp_id;
key_points: Window-function alternative: AVG(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY dept) on each row, then filter
Equivalent CTE/join approach: compute averages once, then join back
Using AVG OVER avoids the need for GROUP BY but cannot be used in WHERE — must wrap in subquery
HR is excluded: 70 is exactly the average, NOT strictly greater

questions.prompt: Schema: `metric(d, v)` is a daily metric (one row per day). For each row, return `d, v, sum_to_date, count_to_date, avg_to_date_2dp` where each metric is computed over all rows with `d <= current d` INCLUDING the current row. Round `avg_to_date_2dp` to 2 decimals. Use window functions (the modern, correct approach), NOT a self-join (which the old SQL pre-2003 approach would have used).
questions.model_answer:
SELECT d, v,
       SUM(v)   OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS sum_to_date,
       COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS count_to_date,
       ROUND(AVG(v) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING)::numeric, 2) AS avg_to_date_2dp
FROM metric
ORDER BY d;
key_points: ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING is critical — explicitly defines a row-based frame from the start to the current row
Without an explicit frame, the default RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW lumps tied ORDER BY values together. Since `d` is unique here, either works, but ROWS is the safer idiom
AVG() OVER returns a float-like type — cast to numeric before ROUND
COUNT(*) over the same frame gives the running count

questions.topic: Top N Per Group with Outer Join
questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(cust_code, cust_name)` and `orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount, ord_date)`. For each customer, return their LARGEST order (by amount, ties broken by earliest date, then ord_num). For customers with no orders, return them with NULL ord fields. Return `cust_code, cust_name, ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date`, ordered by `cust_code`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT cust_code, ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY cust_code ORDER BY ord_amount DESC, ord_date ASC, ord_num ASC) AS rk
  FROM orders
)
SELECT c.cust_code, c.cust_name, r.ord_num, r.ord_amount, r.ord_date
FROM customer c
LEFT JOIN ranked r ON r.cust_code = c.cust_code AND r.rk = 1
ORDER BY c.cust_code;
key_points: ROW_NUMBER with multi-key ORDER BY in the OVER() to deterministically pick one
LEFT JOIN from customer keeps customers with zero orders (NULLs on the order side)
Filter rk = 1 in the JOIN condition (not WHERE) — putting it in WHERE would convert LEFT JOIN to INNER JOIN
Without ord_date and ord_num tie-breakers, C01's choice between ord_num 2 and 3 would be non-deterministic

questions.prompt: Schema: `event(user_id, ts, action)`. For each user, return their FIRST and LAST event (by ts) in ONE row. Return `user_id, first_ts, first_action, last_ts, last_action`, ordered by `user_id`. Use `FIRST_VALUE` / `LAST_VALUE` correctly with explicit frames (the default RANGE frame trap).
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE event (user_id INT, ts TIMESTAMP, action TEXT);
INSERT INTO event VALUES
  (1,'2025-01-01 10:00','signup'),
  (1,'2025-01-02 11:00','view'),
  (1,'2025-01-05 12:00','purchase'),
  (2,'2025-02-01 09:00','signup'),
  (2,'2025-02-10 14:00','churn');
questions.model_answer:
SELECT DISTINCT
  user_id,
  FIRST_VALUE(ts)     OVER w AS first_ts,
  FIRST_VALUE(action) OVER w AS first_action,
  LAST_VALUE(ts)      OVER w AS last_ts,
  LAST_VALUE(action)  OVER w AS last_action
FROM event
WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING)
ORDER BY user_id;
key_points: LAST_VALUE trap: default frame (RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING ... CURRENT ROW) makes LAST_VALUE = current row
Explicit frame ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING gives the partition-wide last
Named WINDOW clause keeps the SELECT clean when multiple columns share the same frame
DISTINCT collapses the per-row window result down to one row per partition

questions.prompt: Schema: `event(user_id, ts)`. Group events into sessions where a NEW session begins after a gap of MORE THAN 60 minutes from the previous event of the same user. Return `user_id, session_id, session_start, session_end, event_count` where `session_id` is the 1-based ordinal within each user. Order by `user_id, session_id`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH flagged AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts,
         CASE WHEN ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) > INTERVAL '60 minutes'
              OR LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) IS NULL
              THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_new
  FROM event
),
numbered AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts,
         SUM(is_new) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS session_id
  FROM flagged
)
SELECT user_id, session_id,
       MIN(ts) AS session_start,
       MAX(ts) AS session_end,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM numbered
GROUP BY user_id, session_id
ORDER BY user_id, session_id;
key_points: LAG to get the previous event's timestamp per user
Mark is_new = 1 when gap > 60 min OR no prior row (LAG returns NULL)
SUM(is_new) OVER (...) gives a stable session_id ordinal within each user
Aggregate to MIN/MAX/COUNT per (user_id, session_id)
Interval comparison: ts - ts yields INTERVAL; compare directly with INTERVAL '60 minutes'

questions.prompt: Generate one row per CALENDAR DAY from `2025-01-01` through `2025-01-10` inclusive, then LEFT JOIN to `signup(user_id, signup_date)` and return `d, signups` (count of signups on that date, 0 if none). Order by `d`. Use Postgres `generate_series`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT g.d::date AS d,
       COUNT(s.user_id) AS signups
FROM generate_series('2025-01-01'::date, '2025-01-10'::date, INTERVAL '1 day') AS g(d)
LEFT JOIN signup s ON s.signup_date = g.d
GROUP BY g.d
ORDER BY g.d;
key_points: generate_series produces the calendar spine (every day, no gaps)
LEFT JOIN preserves zero-signup days
COUNT(s.user_id) returns 0 for unmatched rows (NULL-skipping COUNT)
Cast generate_series output to ::date to match signup_date type

questions.prompt: Schema: `score(category, value)`. Compute the MEDIAN value per category using PERCENTILE_CONT (interpolated median for even-count groups). Return `category, median` rounded to 2 decimals, ordered by `category`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT category,
       ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY value)::numeric, 2) AS median
FROM score
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;
key_points: PERCENTILE_CONT is an ordered-set aggregate: uses WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY ...) syntax
PERCENTILE_CONT INTERPOLATES between values (so the even-count median of {5,15,25,35} = (15+25)/2 = 20)
Use PERCENTILE_DISC if you want a value present in the data (no interpolation)
Postgres returns DOUBLE PRECISION — cast to numeric before ROUND with scale

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(cust_code, cust_name)` and `orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount, ord_date)`. Return each customer's 2 most-recent orders using a Postgres LATERAL subquery (not a window function). Return `cust_code, cust_name, ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date`, ordered by `cust_code, ord_date DESC, ord_num`. Customers with no orders are omitted.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT c.cust_code, c.cust_name, o.ord_num, o.ord_amount, o.ord_date
FROM customer c
JOIN LATERAL (
  SELECT ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date
  FROM orders
  WHERE cust_code = c.cust_code
  ORDER BY ord_date DESC, ord_num DESC
  LIMIT 2
) o ON TRUE
ORDER BY c.cust_code, o.ord_date DESC, o.ord_num;
key_points: LATERAL allows the inner subquery to reference outer columns (c.cust_code) — like a correlated subquery in FROM
ON TRUE because the inner is self-correlated; no join key needed at JOIN level
Inner LIMIT 2 enforces top-N per customer per row
Customers with no orders are dropped because JOIN LATERAL ... ON TRUE has nothing to match (use LEFT JOIN LATERAL to keep them)

questions.prompt: Schema: `monthly(region, month_start, revenue)` (one row per region per first-of-month). For each row, attach the SAME-MONTH-LAST-YEAR revenue and compute YoY growth percent (this/last - 1) * 100, rounded to 2 dp. If there is no same-month-last-year row, both `prev_year_revenue` and `yoy_pct` are NULL. Return `region, month_start, revenue, prev_year_revenue, yoy_pct`, ordered by `region, month_start`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT m.region, m.month_start, m.revenue,
       p.revenue AS prev_year_revenue,
       CASE WHEN p.revenue IS NULL OR p.revenue = 0 THEN NULL
            ELSE ROUND(((m.revenue / p.revenue - 1) * 100)::numeric, 2)
       END AS yoy_pct
FROM monthly m
LEFT JOIN monthly p
  ON p.region = m.region
 AND p.month_start = (m.month_start - INTERVAL '1 year')::date
ORDER BY m.region, m.month_start;
key_points: Self-LEFT-JOIN on (region, month_start - 1 year)
INTERVAL '1 year' is a portable Postgres construct (NOT DATE_SUB which is MySQL)
Cast back to ::date because INTERVAL arithmetic on date produces timestamp
Guard against division by zero / NULL with CASE before ROUND

questions.prompt: Schema: `orders(order_id, status, region, amount)`. In ONE row per region, return total revenue split by status. Statuses are 'paid', 'pending', 'refunded'. Return `region, paid, pending, refunded, total`. Each status column is the SUM of amounts of orders in that status; missing combinations get 0 (not NULL). Order by `region`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT region,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE status = 'paid'), 0) AS paid,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE status = 'pending'), 0) AS pending,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE status = 'refunded'), 0) AS refunded,
       SUM(amount) AS total
FROM orders
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;
key_points: Postgres FILTER (WHERE ...) clause is cleaner than SUM(CASE WHEN ...)
COALESCE NULL aggregates to 0 (a region missing a status will produce NULL otherwise)
SUM(amount) without FILTER gives the unconditional total
Equivalent in pure SQL: SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid' THEN amount ELSE 0 END)

questions.prompt: Schema: `users(user_id)` and `banned(user_id)` where `banned.user_id` MAY contain NULLs (a known data-quality issue). Write a query that correctly returns users who are NOT banned. Demonstrate the NOT-IN NULL pitfall by ALSO returning a count column `naive_count` from the broken query alongside the correct count `safe_count`. Return ONE row with `safe_count, naive_count`.
key_points: NOT IN (subquery with NULL) returns no rows because `x = NULL` is UNKNOWN, and NOT UNKNOWN is UNKNOWN
NOT EXISTS handles NULLs correctly — it tests the negation of EXISTS (a row-by-row predicate)
Alternative safe rewrite: NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM banned WHERE user_id IS NOT NULL)
This is a classic interview gotcha — production NOT IN bugs are common when the inner column allows NULL

questions.prompt: Schema: `tag(item_id, tag, weight)`. For each item, return the comma-separated list of tags ORDERED BY weight DESC (so the most-important tag appears first), with NULL tags skipped. Return `item_id, tag_list`, ordered by `item_id`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT item_id,
       STRING_AGG(tag, ', ' ORDER BY weight DESC) AS tag_list
FROM tag
WHERE tag IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY item_id
ORDER BY item_id;
key_points: Postgres STRING_AGG(col, sep ORDER BY ...) takes ORDER BY inside the aggregate (not in the surrounding query)
WHERE filter strips NULL tags before aggregation (STRING_AGG would silently skip them anyway, but explicit is better)
Item 3 vanishes because it has no non-NULL tags — the GROUP BY collapses, but with all tags filtered the group disappears
MySQL equivalent: GROUP_CONCAT(tag ORDER BY weight DESC SEPARATOR ', ')

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(cust_code, signup_month)` (signup_month is a DATE first-of-month) and `orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_month)` (ord_month also first-of-month). Compute monthly cohort retention: for each cohort (= signup_month), and for each subsequent month, how many customers from that cohort placed at least one order. Return `cohort, ord_month, retained_users`, sorted by cohort then ord_month. Only emit (cohort, ord_month) cells with retained_users >= 1.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT c.signup_month AS cohort,
       o.ord_month,
       COUNT(DISTINCT c.cust_code) AS retained_users
FROM customer c
JOIN orders o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
GROUP BY c.signup_month, o.ord_month
ORDER BY cohort, o.ord_month;
key_points: Join the cohort dimension (customer.signup_month) onto the fact (orders.ord_month) by customer key
COUNT(DISTINCT cust_code) — a customer with multiple orders in the same month should count once
GROUP BY (cohort, ord_month) gives the retention matrix in long format
Reframe to wide format with a pivot if needed for reporting

questions.prompt: Schema: `attendance(emp_id, work_date)`. For each employee, return their LONGEST streak of consecutive workdays (where 'consecutive' means calendar-adjacent dates). Ties: just return the longest length. Return `emp_id, longest_streak`, ordered by `emp_id`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH dt AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT emp_id, work_date FROM attendance
),
grouped AS (
  SELECT emp_id, work_date,
         work_date - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY emp_id ORDER BY work_date))::int AS streak_key
  FROM dt
),
streaks AS (
  SELECT emp_id, streak_key, COUNT(*) AS streak_len
  FROM grouped
  GROUP BY emp_id, streak_key
)
SELECT emp_id, MAX(streak_len) AS longest_streak
FROM streaks
GROUP BY emp_id
ORDER BY emp_id;
key_points: Classic gaps-and-islands: subtract ROW_NUMBER (ordered by date per employee) from the date itself
Consecutive dates produce the same `streak_key` value; gaps reset it
DISTINCT first guards against accidental duplicate rows in attendance
MAX(streak_len) per employee is the longest island

questions.prompt: Schema: `product(product_id, name, sales)`. Compute the Pareto running percentage: order products by sales DESC, and for each row return `cumulative_pct` (running sum / overall total * 100) rounded to 2 dp. Also include a boolean `in_pareto_80` = TRUE for rows that belong to the 'top 80%' set, defined as: every row whose previous cumulative_pct was strictly less than 80 (i.e. the row that takes the curve INTO the 80% zone or further is included, but no row beyond it). Return `product_id, name, sales, cumulative_pct, in_pareto_80`. Order by sales DESC then product_id.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE product (product_id INT, name TEXT, sales NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO product VALUES
  (1,'A',500),
  (2,'B',300),
  (3,'C',100),
  (4,'D',50),
  (5,'E',30),
  (6,'F',20);
questions.model_answer:
WITH cum AS (
  SELECT product_id, name, sales,
         SUM(sales) OVER () AS grand_total,
         SUM(sales) OVER (ORDER BY sales DESC, product_id ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running
  FROM product
),
with_pct AS (
  SELECT product_id, name, sales, grand_total, running,
         ROUND((running / grand_total * 100)::numeric, 2) AS cumulative_pct,
         LAG(running, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY sales DESC, product_id) AS prev_running
  FROM cum
)
SELECT product_id, name, sales, cumulative_pct,
       (prev_running / grand_total * 100 < 80) AS in_pareto_80
FROM with_pct
ORDER BY sales DESC, product_id;
key_points: Window SUM with explicit ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING for the running total
Window SUM with OVER () (no ORDER BY, no PARTITION BY) for the grand total — every row gets the same value
LAG(running, 1, 0) gives the previous row's running total (0 for the first row, via the third LAG argument)
in_pareto_80 = (prev_running / grand_total * 100 < 80) — TRUE while the predecessor was still strictly below 80%, FALSE once we reach or exceed
Row 2 has prev=500 (50%) so in_pareto_80=TRUE; Row 3 has prev=800 (80%), NOT strictly less, so FALSE

questions.prompt: Schema: `category(category_id, parent_id, name)` (a tree). `product(product_id, category_id, name)`. Find all products that belong DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY (via descendant category) to the category named 'Electronics'. Return `product_id, name`, ordered by `product_id`.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE category (category_id INT, parent_id INT, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO category VALUES
  (1,NULL,'All'),
  (2,1,'Electronics'),
  (3,2,'Phones'),
  (4,2,'Laptops'),
  (5,1,'Apparel'),
  (6,5,'Shoes');
CREATE TABLE product (product_id INT, category_id INT, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO product VALUES
  (101,3,'iPhone'),
  (102,3,'Pixel'),
  (103,4,'MacBook'),
  (104,2,'Charger'),
  (105,6,'Sneaker'),
  (106,5,'Tee');
questions.model_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE elec AS (
  SELECT category_id FROM category WHERE name = 'Electronics'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT c.category_id FROM category c JOIN elec e ON c.parent_id = e.category_id
)
SELECT p.product_id, p.name
FROM product p
JOIN elec e ON e.category_id = p.category_id
ORDER BY p.product_id;
key_points: Recursive CTE: anchor = the 'Electronics' node; recurse down via parent_id chain
JOIN product to the descendant set
Filters out Apparel/Shoes and Tee/Sneaker even though they share the same root parent
If 'Electronics' name is ambiguous, anchor by category_id instead

questions.prompt: Schema: `target_products(product_id)` (a curated short list of must-have products) and `cart(user_id, product_id)`. Find users whose cart contains EVERY product in `target_products`. Use the double-NOT-EXISTS formulation (classic SQL relational division). Return `user_id`, ordered ascending.
key_points: Classic double-NOT-EXISTS = 'there does not exist any target product that this user is missing'
Equivalent to: GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT product_id WHERE product_id IN target_products) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM target_products)
Outer DISTINCT collapses the user_id repetitions
Edge: if target_products is empty, the inner NOT EXISTS is vacuously true and EVERY user qualifies

questions.prompt: Schema: `booking(id, room_id, start_ts, end_ts)`. Two bookings overlap if their half-open intervals [start_ts, end_ts) intersect. Find pairs of OVERLAPPING bookings for the SAME room. Return each pair only ONCE (with id_a < id_b). Return `room_id, id_a, id_b`, ordered by `room_id, id_a, id_b`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT a.room_id, a.id AS id_a, b.id AS id_b
FROM booking a JOIN booking b
  ON a.room_id = b.room_id
 AND a.id < b.id
 AND a.start_ts < b.end_ts
 AND b.start_ts < a.end_ts
ORDER BY a.room_id, a.id, b.id;
key_points: Self-join with id_a < id_b avoids reporting (a,b) and (b,a) separately
Half-open overlap formula: a.start < b.end AND b.start < a.end — equivalent to NOT(a.end <= b.start OR b.end <= a.start)
Booking 1 vs 3 NOT counted: 1.end (11:00) is NOT < 3.start (11:00) — half-open boundary
Booking 4 doesn't overlap because it starts at 15:00 and the others all end by 13:00

questions.prompt: Schema: `session(id, login_ts, logout_ts)`. For each given INSTANT in `probe_ts(ts)`, count how many sessions were ACTIVE at that instant (i.e. login_ts <= probe AND logout_ts > probe). Return `ts, active_users`, ordered by `ts`. Include probe rows with zero active users.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT p.ts, COUNT(s.id) AS active_users
FROM probe_ts p
LEFT JOIN session s
  ON s.login_ts <= p.ts AND s.logout_ts > p.ts
GROUP BY p.ts
ORDER BY p.ts;
key_points: Range join (non-equality join) — Postgres handles this with a nested-loop or merge join on the date columns
LEFT JOIN preserves probe rows with zero matches
COUNT(s.id) skips NULLs from the unmatched LEFT JOIN side, naturally returning 0
Be careful with logout boundary semantics: <= vs < at the endpoints determines if a session counts at the moment of logout

questions.prompt: Schema: `basket_item(basket_id, item)`. Find the TOP 3 most-frequent unordered pairs of distinct items that appear together in the same basket. The pair (A, B) where A < B is the same as (B, A). Return `item_a, item_b, frequency`, ordered by `frequency` DESC then `item_a, item_b`. LIMIT 3.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE basket_item (basket_id INT, item TEXT);
INSERT INTO basket_item VALUES
  (1,'bread'),(1,'milk'),(1,'eggs'),
  (2,'bread'),(2,'milk'),
  (3,'bread'),(3,'butter'),
  (4,'milk'),(4,'cookies'),
  (5,'bread'),(5,'milk'),(5,'butter'),
  (6,'bread'),(6,'jam'),
  (7,'bread'),(7,'milk');
questions.model_answer:
SELECT a.item AS item_a, b.item AS item_b, COUNT(*) AS frequency
FROM basket_item a JOIN basket_item b
  ON a.basket_id = b.basket_id AND a.item < b.item
GROUP BY a.item, b.item
ORDER BY frequency DESC, a.item, b.item
LIMIT 3;
key_points: Self-join on basket_id with a.item < b.item to ensure each unordered pair is counted once
Without the a.item < b.item filter, you'd double-count and include trivial (X, X) pairs
Pure SQL approach — most engines handle this without specialized association-rule extensions
For high-cardinality baskets, this is O(n^2) per basket; consider window-function alternatives or pre-filtering

questions.prompt: Schema: `event(user_id, event_type, ts)` where event_type is 'view', 'cart', or 'purchase'. A user 'completed the funnel' if they did each step IN STRICT CHRONOLOGICAL ORDER — i.e. their earliest 'view' came BEFORE their earliest 'cart', which came BEFORE their earliest 'purchase'. Return `user_id` of users who completed the funnel, ordered ascending.
questions.model_answer:
WITH firsts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'view')     AS first_view,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'cart')     AS first_cart,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'purchase') AS first_purchase
  FROM event
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT user_id FROM firsts
WHERE first_view IS NOT NULL
  AND first_cart IS NOT NULL
  AND first_purchase IS NOT NULL
  AND first_view < first_cart
  AND first_cart < first_purchase
ORDER BY user_id;
key_points: FILTER (WHERE event_type = '...') with MIN gives the per-step earliest timestamp
All three timestamps must be non-NULL AND in strict order
User 2 fails: first_cart (09:00) is BEFORE first_view (10:00)
User 3 fails: first_purchase (10:00) is BEFORE first_cart (11:00)
User 4 fails: no purchase at all
User 5 succeeds: even with multiple views, only the EARLIEST matters

questions.prompt: Schema: `customer(cust_code, ltv)`. Bucket customers into DECILES (10 quantile buckets) by LTV — bucket 1 = lowest 10% LTV, bucket 10 = highest 10% LTV. Return `cust_code, ltv, decile`, ordered by `decile` DESC then `ltv` DESC then `cust_code`.
questions.model_answer:
SELECT cust_code, ltv,
       NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY ltv) AS decile
FROM customer
ORDER BY decile DESC, ltv DESC, cust_code;
key_points: NTILE(N) splits the partition into N approximately-equal buckets ordered by the OVER's ORDER BY
When the row count is not evenly divisible by N, the EARLIER buckets get one more row
Lowest values get bucket 1 (with ASC ORDER BY); use ORDER BY DESC to invert
For VERY skewed distributions consider PERCENT_RANK instead — NTILE doesn't respect value gaps

questions.prompt: Schema: `status_log(entity_id, ts, status)` is an append-only log of state changes. For each entity, return its CURRENT (latest by ts) status. Tie-break: later `status` alphabetically wins (unlikely in practice but deterministic). Use Postgres `DISTINCT ON`. Return `entity_id, ts, status`, ordered by `entity_id`.
key_points: DISTINCT ON (col) keeps the FIRST row per group, where 'first' is determined by ORDER BY
ORDER BY MUST start with the DISTINCT ON columns
For entity 3 with two ties at the same ts, the secondary ORDER BY status DESC picks 'reopened' over 'open'
Portable equivalent: window-then-filter with ROW_NUMBER OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY ts DESC, status DESC) = 1

questions.prompt:
You want all customers who have NEVER placed an order. You write:

```sql
SELECT cust_code FROM customer
WHERE cust_code NOT IN (SELECT cust_code FROM orders);
```

The orders table has a row with `cust_code = NULL` (a data-quality artifact). What is the most likely outcome?
options: Returns customers who have never placed an order — the NULL is ignored
Returns ZERO rows because `x NOT IN (..., NULL, ...)` evaluates to UNKNOWN for every customer
Returns ALL customers because Postgres ignores NULL in subqueries
Throws a runtime error because of the NULL in the subquery
questions.explanation: Classic NOT IN + NULL bug. `x NOT IN (subquery)` is rewritten as `x != v1 AND x != v2 AND ... AND x != NULL`. The last term is UNKNOWN. AND with UNKNOWN propagates, so the predicate is never TRUE for any row, and the query returns ZERO rows. Two safe fixes: (a) NOT EXISTS, which uses row-by-row semantics and is NULL-safe; (b) NOT IN (SELECT cust_code FROM orders WHERE cust_code IS NOT NULL).

questions.prompt: Schema: `employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT)`. For EACH department, compute the gap between the HIGHEST and SECOND-HIGHEST distinct salary. Departments where only one distinct salary exists (i.e. no genuine second-place value) must surface a NULL gap rather than 0 or being dropped. Return columns `dept, top_salary, second_salary, gap`, ordered by `dept`. Use DENSE_RANK so ties at #1 still allow the next distinct salary to be #2.
questions.model_answer:
WITH d AS (
  SELECT dept, salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
  FROM employee
  GROUP BY dept, salary
)
SELECT dept,
       MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 1) AS top_salary,
       MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 2) AS second_salary,
       (MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 1) - MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 2)) AS gap
FROM d
WHERE rk <= 2
GROUP BY dept
ORDER BY dept;
key_points: DENSE_RANK on DISTINCT salaries so ties at #1 share rank, second-place is the next distinct value
FILTER (WHERE rk = N) is the Postgres-native conditional aggregate
Departments with a single distinct salary must keep the row but with NULL second_salary/gap
Arithmetic with NULL naturally produces NULL gap — no special-casing needed

questions.prompt: Schema: `orders(customer_id INT, order_date DATE, amount NUMERIC)`. Find customers who have at least one run of THREE OR MORE STRICTLY INCREASING consecutive orders (ordered by `order_date`). That means there exists some position N for the customer such that amount[N] < amount[N+1] < amount[N+2]. Return distinct `customer_id` ordered ascending. Two-LAG comparison only — do not use recursive CTE or self-join.
questions.model_answer:
WITH w AS (
  SELECT customer_id,
         amount,
         LAG(amount, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev1,
         LAG(amount, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev2
  FROM orders
)
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id
FROM w
WHERE prev2 IS NOT NULL
  AND prev2 < prev1
  AND prev1 < amount
ORDER BY customer_id;
key_points: LAG(amount, 1) and LAG(amount, 2) give you the two prior amounts — sufficient to detect a 3-row strict increase
Customer 2 has values 50,40,60,80 — at row 4 prev2=40, prev1=60, amount=80, so 40<60<80 is a valid 3-row strict increase even though row 2 was a dip
Customer 3 has 10→10→20 — first step is equal, NOT strictly increasing, so excluded
STRICT inequality (<, not <=) — equal values break the run

questions.prompt: Schema: `employee(emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, manager_id INT)` — CEO has manager_id NULL. Given two specific employees with `emp_id` = 6 and `emp_id` = 7, return the LOWEST COMMON MANAGER (the deepest node that is an ancestor of BOTH). Use a recursive CTE to compute each employee's full ancestor chain (including themselves), then intersect. Return columns `lca_emp_id, lca_name`. If one of the employees is itself the ancestor of the other, return that employee.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, manager_id INT);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),
  (3,'Carla',1),
  (4,'Dan',2),
  (5,'Erin',2),
  (6,'Finn',4),
  (7,'Gita',5),
  (8,'Hugo',3);
questions.model_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE ancestors AS (
  SELECT emp_id AS start_id, emp_id AS anc_id, 0 AS depth
  FROM employee
  UNION ALL
  SELECT a.start_id, e.manager_id, a.depth + 1
  FROM ancestors a
  JOIN employee e ON e.emp_id = a.anc_id
  WHERE e.manager_id IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT a1.anc_id AS lca_emp_id, e.name AS lca_name
FROM ancestors a1
JOIN ancestors a2 ON a1.anc_id = a2.anc_id
JOIN employee e ON e.emp_id = a1.anc_id
WHERE a1.start_id = 6 AND a2.start_id = 7
ORDER BY a1.depth ASC
LIMIT 1;
key_points: Recursive CTE walks UPWARD via manager_id from each employee, recording depth
Include the employee itself at depth 0 (an employee is its own ancestor for LCA purposes)
Intersect both ancestor sets and pick the one with SMALLEST depth from emp 6 (which equals deepest in the tree)
LIMIT 1 after sorting by depth ASC gives the lowest (closest) common ancestor

questions.prompt: Schema: `salary_history(emp_id INT, effective_date DATE, salary INT)` records salary changes over time. For each employee, NTILE-bucket their CHRONOLOGICAL salary points into 3 'compensation tiers' (1=lowest third, 3=highest third). Return only the rows where the tier STRICTLY INCREASED from the previous chronological row for that employee (a tier transition upward). Output columns: `emp_id, effective_date, salary, prev_tier, new_tier`. Order by `emp_id, effective_date`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH t AS (
  SELECT emp_id, effective_date, salary,
         NTILE(3) OVER (PARTITION BY emp_id ORDER BY salary) AS tier
  FROM salary_history
),
lag_t AS (
  SELECT emp_id, effective_date, salary, tier,
         LAG(tier) OVER (PARTITION BY emp_id ORDER BY effective_date) AS prev_tier
  FROM t
)
SELECT emp_id, effective_date, salary, prev_tier, tier AS new_tier
FROM lag_t
WHERE prev_tier IS NOT NULL AND tier > prev_tier
ORDER BY emp_id, effective_date;
key_points: NTILE(3) is ordered by SALARY (so tier numbers reflect compensation rank), NOT by date
LAG(tier) over chronological order gives the previous tier — second window pass
Filter prev_tier IS NOT NULL (drop first row) AND new_tier > prev_tier (strict upward transition)
Gotcha: doing NTILE over date and then comparing tier would be a no-op (tiers would always increase)

questions.prompt: Schema: `monthly_revenue(month_start DATE, revenue NUMERIC)` — one row per first-of-month. Find the FIRST month (chronologically) at which MoM growth EXCEEDED 20% AND the PRIOR month also had positive growth (i.e. two consecutive months of growth, with the second crossing the 20% threshold). Return columns `month_start, prev_revenue, revenue, mom_growth_pct` for that single row. mom_growth_pct rounded to 2 decimals. If no such month exists, return zero rows.
questions.model_answer:
WITH g AS (
  SELECT month_start, revenue,
         LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month_start) AS prev_revenue,
         LAG(revenue, 2) OVER (ORDER BY month_start) AS prev2_revenue
  FROM monthly_revenue
)
SELECT month_start,
       prev_revenue,
       revenue,
       ROUND(((revenue - prev_revenue) / NULLIF(prev_revenue, 0) * 100)::numeric, 2) AS mom_growth_pct
FROM g
WHERE prev_revenue IS NOT NULL AND prev2_revenue IS NOT NULL
  AND prev_revenue > prev2_revenue
  AND (revenue - prev_revenue) / NULLIF(prev_revenue, 0) > 0.20
ORDER BY month_start
LIMIT 1;
key_points: Two LAG offsets (1 and 2 months) capture the immediate prior and one-before-that months
Need BOTH conditions: (a) prev_revenue > prev2_revenue (prior month also grew); (b) current/prev - 1 > 0.20
ORDER BY month_start LIMIT 1 returns just the first qualifying month
NULLIF guards divide-by-zero; rows with insufficient history (prev2_revenue NULL) are excluded

questions.prompt: Schema: `employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT)`. Find 'underperformers in elite departments': employees who are in the TOP HALF of company-wide salary (CUME_DIST > 0.50) BUT in the BOTTOM 30% of their own department (CUME_DIST <= 0.30 within dept). Return `emp_id, dept, salary, company_pct, dept_pct` where the two percentiles are rounded to 2 decimals. Order by `emp_id`. (This catches people who are well-paid by global standards but lagging within an elite team.)
questions.model_answer:
WITH r AS (
  SELECT emp_id, dept, salary,
         CUME_DIST() OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS company_pct,
         CUME_DIST() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary) AS dept_pct
  FROM employee
)
SELECT emp_id, dept, salary,
       ROUND(company_pct::numeric, 2) AS company_pct,
       ROUND(dept_pct::numeric, 2) AS dept_pct
FROM r
WHERE company_pct > 0.50 AND dept_pct <= 0.30
ORDER BY emp_id;
key_points: CUME_DIST returns fraction of rows AT OR BELOW the current value — appropriate for 'within top/bottom percentile' framing
Two CUME_DIST window passes: company-wide (no PARTITION) and dept-scoped
Strict > 0.70 for company top-30% (matches 'top of upper third' framing)
<= 0.30 for dept bottom-30% (inclusive of the 30th percentile boundary)

questions.prompt: Schema: `employee(emp_id INT, name TEXT, manager_id INT)`. For each manager, return the count of their 'skip-level reports' — employees who report to someone who reports to them (i.e. EXACTLY 2 levels below them in the tree, NOT direct reports, NOT 3+ levels down). Return `manager_id, manager_name, skip_level_count`, ordered by `manager_id`. Only include managers who have at least one skip-level report.
questions.schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (emp_id INT, name TEXT, manager_id INT);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),
  (3,'Carla',1),
  (4,'Dan',2),
  (5,'Erin',2),
  (6,'Finn',3),
  (7,'Gita',4),
  (8,'Hugo',5),
  (9,'Iris',7);
questions.model_answer:
SELECT m.emp_id AS manager_id,
       m.name AS manager_name,
       COUNT(skip.emp_id)::int AS skip_level_count
FROM employee m
JOIN employee mid ON mid.manager_id = m.emp_id
JOIN employee skip ON skip.manager_id = mid.emp_id
GROUP BY m.emp_id, m.name
ORDER BY m.emp_id;
key_points: Skip-level = exactly 2 hops down. Two consecutive INNER JOINs on manager_id give the 2-hop set directly
Counts only managers with ≥ 1 skip-level report (GROUP BY drops empty managers because INNER JOIN filtered them)
Does NOT require recursion — fixed depth lookup. A recursive CTE would over-engineer this and risk including 3+ level descendants
Alice's skip-levels: Dan, Erin (under Bob), Finn (under Carla) = 3

questions.prompt: Schema: `sale(sale_id INT, salesperson_id INT, team_id INT, amount NUMERIC)`. A `team_id` may have a 10000 commission pool to split among its salespeople, proportional to each salesperson's TOTAL sales within the team. Compute each salesperson's share of the 10000 pool, rounded to 2 decimals. Output columns `team_id, salesperson_id, total_sales, commission_share`. Order by `team_id, salesperson_id`. A salesperson may appear in multiple teams — keep them as separate rows per team.
questions.model_answer:
WITH per_person AS (
  SELECT team_id, salesperson_id, SUM(amount) AS total_sales
  FROM sale
  GROUP BY team_id, salesperson_id
)
SELECT team_id,
       salesperson_id,
       total_sales,
       ROUND((10000.0 * total_sales / SUM(total_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY team_id))::numeric, 2) AS commission_share
FROM per_person
ORDER BY team_id, salesperson_id;
key_points: Two-stage: first pre-aggregate per (team, salesperson) to one row each, THEN apply the window
SUM(total_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY team_id) gives the team total without a second join
Salesperson 101 appears in both team 1 and team 2 with different shares — that's intentional, two rows
Single-person team (105) takes the full 10000 pool

questions.prompt: Schema: `event(user_id INT, event_date DATE)`. Build a cohort retention table: each user's COHORT MONTH is their first-ever event month. For each (cohort_month, months_since_cohort) pair where months_since_cohort is 0, 1, 2, or 3, return the count of cohort users who had ANY event in the calendar month exactly that many months after their cohort month. Output columns: `cohort_month, months_since, active_users`. Include zero counts for in-range cells. Order by `cohort_month, months_since`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH first_event AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(event_date))::date AS cohort_month
  FROM event
  GROUP BY user_id
),
active AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', event_date)::date AS active_month
  FROM event
),
grid AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT f.cohort_month, m.months_since
  FROM first_event f
  CROSS JOIN (VALUES (0),(1),(2),(3)) AS m(months_since)
)
SELECT g.cohort_month,
       g.months_since,
       COUNT(DISTINCT a.user_id)::int AS active_users
FROM grid g
LEFT JOIN first_event f ON f.cohort_month = g.cohort_month
LEFT JOIN active a
  ON a.user_id = f.user_id
  AND a.active_month = (g.cohort_month + (g.months_since || ' months')::interval)::date
GROUP BY g.cohort_month, g.months_since
ORDER BY g.cohort_month, g.months_since;
key_points: Three-stage CTE: first_event (cohort assignment), active (per-user-month presence), grid (Cartesian of cohorts × offsets)
LEFT JOIN the grid to ensure (cohort, months_since) cells with zero retention still appear
Offset arithmetic: `cohort_month + (n || ' months')::interval` jumps to the target month-start cleanly
COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) — users may have multiple events in the same calendar month

questions.topic: Window Functions / Running vs Static Aggregate
questions.prompt: Schema: `sale(sale_id INT, sale_date DATE, amount NUMERIC)`. For each sale, compute its RUNNING average up to and including that row, AND the GLOBAL average across the whole table. Then return ONLY the sales where the running average is FOR THE FIRST TIME (chronologically) at or above the global average. There is at most one such row by definition of 'first time'. Columns: `sale_date, amount, running_avg, global_avg`. running_avg and global_avg rounded to 2 decimals.
questions.model_answer:
WITH base AS (
  SELECT sale_date, amount,
         AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_avg,
         AVG(amount) OVER () AS global_avg
  FROM sale
),
flagged AS (
  SELECT sale_date, amount, running_avg, global_avg,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY (running_avg >= global_avg) ORDER BY sale_date) AS first_above_rn
  FROM base
  WHERE running_avg >= global_avg
)
SELECT sale_date,
       amount,
       ROUND(running_avg::numeric, 2) AS running_avg,
       ROUND(global_avg::numeric, 2) AS global_avg
FROM flagged
WHERE first_above_rn = 1
ORDER BY sale_date;
key_points: Two window passes in one SELECT: a running AVG (ROWS frame, not the default RANGE) and a global AVG (empty OVER)
Explicit ROWS frame is required — default RANGE with ORDER BY produces the same result here only because dates are unique, but ROWS makes intent explicit
Filter rows where running_avg >= global_avg, then ROW_NUMBER ordered by sale_date to keep only the FIRST such crossing
Running avg trajectory: 10, 15, 15, 31.25, 37 (still below 37.14), 38.33 (FIRST crossing), 37.14

questions.topic: Window Functions / Ranked Top-1 With Department Tie
questions.prompt: Schema: `employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT)`. Find the department(s) that contain the LARGEST number of employees whose salary equals the highest salary in their own department. (For each dept, count how many people TIE for the dept's max salary — return the dept(s) with the largest such count; tie at the dept level keeps all winners.) Return columns: `dept, top_earner_count`, ordered by `dept`.
questions.model_answer:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT dept, salary,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
  FROM employee
),
per_dept AS (
  SELECT dept, COUNT(*) AS top_earner_count
  FROM ranked
  WHERE rk = 1
  GROUP BY dept
)
SELECT dept, top_earner_count
FROM per_dept
